clusters.subject: Enseignement scientifique
clusters.domain: 3.5 — De la machine de Turing à l'intelligence artificielle
clusters.summary: Your teenager gets a clear-eyed look at how computers and artificial intelligence really work, from Turing's idea of a universal machine to today's systems that learn patterns from huge amounts of data. They learn why data quality matters and how bias creeps in, why a correlation is not a cause, and what ethical questions AI raises. In a world full of AI hype, it gives them the grounding to judge it for themselves.

clusters.subject: Enseignement scientifique
clusters.summary: Your teenager discovers the physics hidden inside music: how a guitar string's length and tension set its pitch, why notes an octave apart sound related, and how any sound breaks down into simpler pure tones. They also learn how loudness is measured in decibels on a scale that grows surprisingly fast. It reveals the science behind sounds they already love.

clusters.subject: Enseignement scientifique
clusters.domain: 4.2 — Le son, une information à coder
clusters.summary: Your teenager learns how a sound gets turned into the digital files on their phone: sampling the wave many times a second and rounding each value into numbers a computer can store. They see how compression shrinks files by dropping details the ear barely notices, and even weigh the energy cost of streaming and sharing all that audio and video. It connects everyday listening to how digital technology actually works.

clusters.subject: Enseignement scientifique
clusters.summary: Your teenager learns how hearing works, from the eardrum passing vibrations inward to the delicate hair cells in the inner ear that turn sound into nerve signals for the brain. Crucially, those hair cells are few, fragile, and do not grow back, so loud or long exposure can cause permanent damage. It is practical science that helps them protect their own hearing for life.

clusters.subject: Enseignement scientifique
clusters.summary: Your teenager carries out a real hands-on science project: measuring something for themselves, often with digital sensors, then processing the data and judging critically what it shows. Rather than just reading about science, they get to practise the full experimental method from measurement to interpretation. It turns everything they have studied into genuine scientific doing.

clusters.subject: Explorer le monde
clusters.domain: Explorer le monde du vivant, des objets et de la matière
clusters.summary: Your child is becoming a young explorer of the world around them, learning what makes something alive by caring for classroom animals and plants and watching them grow, change and die. They use their senses to sort and compare, shape and mix materials with their own hands, name the parts of their body, and pick up simple habits of hygiene, safety and caring for living things. This hands-on curiosity is how young children first make sense of nature and everyday objects, and it lays the groundwork for the science they will meet later.

clusters.subject: Explorer le monde
clusters.domain: Se repérer dans le temps et l'espace
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to find their bearings in time and space: naming the day of the week, sensing that some things take longer than others, and putting pictures of a story or a day in the right order. They also learn to describe where things are using words like 'devant', 'derrière' and 'dessus', to follow a simple map or plan, and to see the world from someone else's point of view. These early time and space markers give children the structure they need to organise their thinking and, later, to read maps, timelines and history.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning why French words change their spelling depending on the words around them: making verbs agree with their subject, matching adjectives and nouns in gender and number, and getting verb endings right across the main tenses. This is the reasoning behind so many silent spelling choices, and it turns careful writing into a habit rather than guesswork.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is memorizing how individual words are spelled, from small everyday words that never change to trickier ones, by noticing patterns in how words are built and where they come from. Storing spellings this way lets them write more fluently without stopping to puzzle over each word.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Adopter une attitude critique par rapport à son propos
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to step back and judge their own speaking: listening to a recording of a presentation, spotting what could be clearer, and reworking it with classmates' feedback. They also start to notice how talking out loud differs from writing, which sharpens both.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is reading epics, novels and news to explore how stories and information shape our view of the world, from the deeds of heroes to how a news report is actually put together. It builds both a love of powerful storytelling and a healthy, questioning eye toward the media around them.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Analyser le fonctionnement de la phrase simple et de la phrase complexe
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to take a sentence apart to see how it works: naming its building blocks, spotting different kinds of complements, and understanding how clauses combine into longer, more sophisticated sentences. This deeper grasp of structure helps them read demanding texts and write with more control.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to identify the main parts of a sentence, the subject, the verb and the complements, and to use simple tests like moving or removing words to check each one's role. Seeing how sentences are built underpins both clearer writing and more accurate spelling.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your young child is getting their hand ready to write: strengthening finger control, learning to hold a pencil properly, tracing basic lines and shapes, and moving from capital letters toward joined-up cursive. At this age it is very physical work, and mastering the gesture now makes writing feel natural later.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Apprendre à écrire en écriture cursive
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to write in cursive, forming each joined-up letter with the right shape and stroke, linking them smoothly into words, and recognizing the same letter across its different printed and handwritten forms. Daily practice turns handwriting into a comfortable, automatic skill.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Approfondir sa maîtrise de la conjugaison
clusters.summary: Your child is deepening their command of verb conjugation: understanding how endings are built, handling compound tenses like the passé composé, and getting past-participle agreement right. These are among the trickiest spelling decisions in French, and steady practice makes them dependable.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is building strong reading habits, reading aloud with expression, reading silently to themselves, tackling longer texts of several pages, and keeping a personal record of what they read. Reading widely and regularly at this age is what turns a decoder into a real reader.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Ce qui est attendu des enfants en fin d'école maternelle
clusters.summary: By the end of nursery school, your child is expected to speak in clear, correct sentences, use the words learned in class, and make themselves understood by adults and other children. They are also growing curious about writing and beginning to see that spoken and written language are not the same.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Comprendre des textes, des documents et des images, et les interpréter
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to work out who is speaking in a document and where it comes from, and to make sense of pages that mix text, images and captions. Being able to sort and question different kinds of material is a key skill for learning across every subject.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to speak clearly enough to be heard and understood, to join in class conversations, and to take a step back and check their own speaking against shared rules. Confident, considerate talking is the foundation for so much of school life.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to speak in a controlled way to an audience, managing their delivery, using the voice expressively, and building constructively on what others say in a discussion. These are the speaking skills that carry into presentations and debates.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Comprendre le fonctionnement de la langue
clusters.summary: Your child is starting to see how French works as a system: how sounds map to letters, how a simple sentence is built, and the first spelling agreements for gender, number and common words. Grasping these patterns early makes both reading and writing more secure.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to truly understand what they read, not just decode it, picturing the story in their mind, tracking who is who, working out characters' feelings, and noticing when meaning slips so they can reread. These active strategies are what turn reading into real understanding.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Comprendre un texte littéraire et se l'approprier
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to reach the meaning of a literary text more independently, using reading methods learned in class and recognizing a poem, a play or a story just from how it is laid out on the page. They are also introduced to how verb tenses shape the way a story is told.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Connaître les différences entre l'oral et l'écrit
clusters.summary: Your child is examining how spoken and written French differ, from features like liaison to the gap in register between casual talk and polished writing, and learning to move between the two. Thinking about language this way lets them choose the right level for each situation.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Connaître les trois types de phrases et leurs formes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning the three sentence types, statements, questions and commands, along with the negative and exclamative forms, and practising turning one into another. Recognizing these shapes helps them read for tone and write with intention.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your young child is learning to hear the sounds inside spoken words: clapping out syllables, noticing rhymes, and picking out individual sounds. This ear for sounds is one of the most important foundations for learning to read.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is turning spelling into reliable reflexes: mastering how agreement spreads through a sentence, conjugating the full verb system across moods and tenses, and using word origins to spell confidently. This is where spelling shifts from rules they think about to habits they can trust.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Construire les notions permettant l'analyse et l'élaboration des textes et des discours
clusters.summary: Your child is learning the tools that hold longer texts together: active and passive voice, reported speech, connectors and reference words, and who is speaking to whom. Understanding how a text is constructed helps them both analyze what they read and organize what they write.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Contrôler sa compréhension, devenir un lecteur autonome
clusters.summary: Your child is becoming a self-reliant reader, adjusting how they read to their purpose, reading between the lines, backing up interpretations with evidence, and choosing books for themselves. The goal is a reader who monitors their own understanding and reads by genuine choice.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Copier et acquérir des stratégies de copie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to copy quickly and accurately, moving beyond letter-by-letter to holding whole words in mind, keeping the layout of the original, and rereading to catch mistakes. Efficient, fluent copying frees up attention for everything else they write.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is building a personal literary culture through a rich mix of stories, poems and plays, meeting heroes and monsters, exploring myths and origin tales, and savouring the taste of poetic language. These encounters open up human complexity and let them imagine lives far from their own.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is becoming a reader who reads for pleasure, choosing books by interest, reading whole works of many kinds across the year, linking one book to another, and sharing what they read with classmates. Building these habits and a shared literary heritage is what makes reading stick.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to speak so an audience really follows them, reciting a poem clearly, giving a short prepared talk, using linking words to structure their ideas, and judging their own performance to improve it. This is confident, organized speaking in front of others.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Découvrir, comprendre et mettre en œuvre l'orthographe grammaticale
clusters.summary: Your child is meeting the first grammar-of-spelling rules: how a determiner, noun and adjective agree within a group, how verbs change with their subject, and the marks for feminine and plural. Talking these choices through out loud helps the reasoning become automatic.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your young child is building richer spoken sentences, using more tenses to talk about the past and future, widening the small words like pronouns they use, and linking ideas into longer sentences. Growing this spoken structure now supports everything they will later read and write.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to write words from their sounds, breaking speech into syllables and sounds, choosing the right letters, spelling frequent words, and taking dictation. This encoding is the writing half of learning to read, and both grow together.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is growing a richer, better-organized vocabulary, exploring how word meanings relate and shift, grouping words into families and fields, and understanding how words are built from roots, prefixes and suffixes. A deeper word store makes both their reading and their writing more precise.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is expanding their vocabulary and learning how words are made, spotting prefixes, suffixes and Latin or Greek roots, grouping words into families, and using a dictionary well. Reading widely and reusing new words in their own writing is what makes vocabulary grow.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your young child is learning and reusing new words drawn from home, class and stories, sorting them into categories, spotting the odd one out, and beginning to notice that a word can have more than one meaning. A rich early vocabulary is one of the strongest signs of later reading success.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Enrichir son vocabulaire dans toutes les disciplines
clusters.summary: Your child is building vocabulary across every subject, adding set phrases, using a dictionary, working out meaning from a word's parts, and noticing when a word is used literally or figuratively. Words learned in context, everywhere in the school day, are the ones that stick.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Exploiter les principales fonctions de l'écrit
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering what writing is for: as a tool to think and learn, as a way to reach a reader in many different forms, and as something whose role has changed across history. Seeing writing as more than schoolwork makes it a genuinely useful skill.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Exploiter les ressources expressives et créatives de la parole
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring the expressive power of the voice, reciting and performing texts aloud, using breath, gaze and gesture to bring words to life, and even recording their own multimedia pieces. It builds both stage confidence and a feel for how delivery shapes meaning.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to argue with nuance: stating an opinion and then qualifying it, refuting someone else's view, and using the language of cause, consequence, comparison and condition to build a logical case. Structuring an argument clearly is a backbone skill for the lycée and beyond.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is mastering the linguistic tools of reasoning, the connectors of contrast, cause, consequence and condition, while learning to state and defend an opinion and to condense a text faithfully into fewer words. This includes the contraction de texte, a key written exam exercise.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is consolidating the grammar that keeps writing accurate: reliable agreement, how clauses combine in complex sentences, relative clauses, and what verb tenses and moods convey about time and attitude. These are the core skills behind correct, controlled writing.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying grammar as a source of meaning and effect: how French builds negation and questions, how subordinate clauses are assembled, and how verb tenses and their sequence keep a text coherent. The focus is on what these choices do, not just on naming them.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Identifier les constituants d'une phrase simple
clusters.summary: Your child is consolidating how to find the key parts of a sentence: the conjugated verb, the subject in its various forms, the noun group and its head noun, and the difference between object and circumstantial complements. This solid analysis underpins accurate agreement and spelling.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Identifier les constituants d'une phrase simple / Se repérer dans la phrase complexe
clusters.summary: Your child is deepening sentence analysis, telling apart different complements, distinguishing a word's grammatical class from its role in the sentence, and taking first steps into complex sentences with coordination and subordination. It is the groundwork for handling longer, richer sentences.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Identifier les mots de manière de plus en plus aisée
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to recognize written words more and more easily, matching letters to sounds and blending them, reading frequent words instantly on sight, and decoding brand-new words fluently. Fast, accurate word recognition is what frees the mind to focus on meaning.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Identifier les mots selon leur nature / classe grammaticale
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to name the different classes of words, determiners, pronouns, conjunctions, prepositions and adverbs, and to see how pronouns stand in for earlier words. Knowing what each word is helps them understand how sentences hold together.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your young child is learning to use spoken language for real purposes, daring to speak up, building clearer sentences, learning new words, reciting rhymes, and rephrasing to be understood. At this age, talking is the main tool for expressing themselves, understanding and thinking.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your young child is discovering what writing is: that written signs carry language, that a text says the same thing each time, and that letters can be combined to make words. They are also learning to recognize and write their own first name, an exciting first taste of authorship.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: La littérature d'idées du XVIe siècle au XVIIIe siècle
clusters.summary: Your teenager is reading the great texts of ideas from the Renaissance to the Enlightenment for their reasoning, following an argument's movement, learning the classical genres of persuasive speech, running structured debates, and writing essays. It sharpens both critical thinking and formal argumentative writing.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: La littérature d'idées et la presse du XIXe au XXIe siècle
clusters.summary: Your teenager is exploring the literature of ideas and the press across the last two centuries, studying great ethical and social debates, learning to spot an argument's aim and its unstated assumptions, and forming independent critical judgment. They also practise text contraction and argumentative writing.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: La poésie du Moyen Âge au XVIIIe siècle
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying poetry from the Middle Ages to the 18th century, reading it aloud with attention to its rhythm and sound, placing it in literary history, and learning the demanding commentaire and explication de texte exercises. They also respond creatively by writing poems of their own.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is developing spoken language, listening attentively, speaking clearly to suit topic and audience, and practising forms like narrating, describing and explaining. Confident listening and speaking underpin learning in every subject.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is strengthening their oral skills, giving a short prepared presentation with notes, listening to a story and showing they understood it, joining discussions constructively, and reciting from memory. Speaking up clearly and sharing a point of view is a core skill at this age.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to sustain a five-to-ten-minute talk, take part in a debate while respecting others' turns, and understand elaborate spoken material like a lecture or documentary. These are the more demanding listening and speaking skills of collège.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Le roman et le récit du XVIIIe au XXIe siècle
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying the novel and narrative from the 18th to the 21st century, reading complete works, situating them historically, and learning the dissertation, a structured argumentative essay on a literary question. They also read a further novel independently and respond in their own creative writing.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Le théâtre du XVIIe au XXIe siècle
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying theatre from the 17th to the 21st century, reading and performing dialogue aloud, understanding a play as a text meant to be staged, and placing works in the history of the genre. Acting and improvisation build both expressive skill and confidence.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to read and understand, reading daily both aloud and silently on ever harder material, working through several complete books a year, and checking their own understanding as they go. This is where decoding and meaning come together.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is reading a set number of complete works each year, mixing youth literature with heritage classics, and using texts, tables and diagrams to learn across every subject. Reading, understanding and interpreting increasingly demanding literature is central at this age.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Lecture et compréhension de l'écrit et de l'image
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to read and interpret literary texts with simple analytical tools, to read a broad range of works each year, and to make sense of increasingly complex texts, images and mixed documents on their own. Reading a full work and reporting on it becomes a regular expectation.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is preparing for the French baccalauréat exercises: close textual explanation, the literary commentary and the dissertation, expressive oral reading, the oral presentation, and independent wider reading. Together these build deep, methodical engagement with literature.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is building reading fluency, decoding automatically so no effort is left over for it, reading a full page smoothly, chunking sentences into meaningful groups, and reading aloud with expression. Reaching a good reading speed frees all their attention for understanding.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Lire des textes non littéraires, des images et des documents composites (y compris numériques)
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to read beyond stories, analyzing images, knowing the features of articles, essays and diagrams, and making sense of documents that combine text and image on paper and screen. These are essential skills for navigating information critically.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Lire et comprendre des textes, des documents et des images pour apprendre dans toutes les disciplines
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to read in order to learn, finding and combining information from several documents, checking where a document comes from, comparing sources, and interpreting images. Being able to draw on many kinds of material supports learning across the whole curriculum.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Lire et comprendre seul des textes, des documents et des images
clusters.summary: Your child is becoming an independent reader, building a toolkit of comprehension strategies, telling apart what a text states outright from what it only implies, and backing up interpretations with evidence. The aim is to grasp the overall meaning of longer, harder texts on their own.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Lire une œuvre et se l'approprier
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to make a book their own, sticking with a longer work, linking it to their own life and other reading, building a detailed understanding grounded in the text, and sharing and debating their impressions. It nurtures both careful reading and genuine reading pleasure.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to read aloud well, voicing decoded words and sentences, respecting punctuation and grouping words into breath units, and reaching the year's reading-speed goals with growing expression. Reading aloud is both a skill in itself and proof that reading is becoming fluent.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Lire à voix haute avec expressivité
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to bring a text to life aloud, controlling their voice and pace, working on intonation, conveying characters' emotions and the author's intent, and giving different voices to speakers in dialogue. Reading expressively shows deep understanding and is a real pleasure to share.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Maîtriser les relations entre l'oral et l'écrit
clusters.summary: Your child is mastering how spoken French maps onto writing, knowing all the sounds and the letters that spell them, handling the marks for gender and number, and telling apart words that sound alike but are spelled differently, like a and à. This bridge between sound and spelling underlies accurate writing.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is memorizing how words are spelled, noticing accents, explaining silent final letters by looking at related words, understanding that letters like c and g change sound with context, and using word families to spell faster. Building this store of spellings makes writing smoother.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Parler en prenant en compte son auditoire
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to speak with their audience in mind, using voice and body to hold attention, structuring a talk to fit its purpose, performing poems and plays aloud, and gradually speaking from notes rather than a full script. It is confident, deliberate public speaking.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Participer de façon constructive à des échanges oraux
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to debate constructively, stating an argued opinion and defending it, genuinely taking account of what others say, and even chairing a discussion to keep it orderly. These are the skills of respectful, productive group exchange.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to take part in conversations, contributing on topic, taking turns and listening, giving a short prepared talk, and noticing that we speak differently in class than in the playground. Learning to converse fairly and clearly is an important social skill.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to hold their own in discussion, adjusting how they speak to the situation, building on others' points, beginning to reason and argue, and respecting the turn-taking rules of conversation. They are also learning polite phrases for requesting, refusing and thanking.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Passer du recours intuitif à l'argumentation à un usage plus maîtrisé
clusters.summary: Your teenager is moving from arguing on instinct to arguing with method, learning what makes discourse persuasive, ranking arguments and backing them with examples, and telling apart convincing through reason from persuading through emotion. It is the foundation of structured argumentative writing.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Prendre en compte les normes de l'écrit pour formuler, transcrire et réviser
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to write to the standards of written French, applying spelling and agreement rules, punctuating with purpose, organizing a text into paragraphs, and hunting for likely mistakes to revise. The goal is a legible, coherent, corrected final text.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your young child is discovering the alphabetic principle, that writing mostly codes the sounds of speech, while learning the names of letters, recognizing them in their different forms, and telling apart look-alikes like b and d. This is a decisive step toward reading and writing.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Produire de premiers écrits — Passer de l'oral à l'écrit
clusters.summary: Your young child is taking their first steps from speech to writing, dictating a message to an adult to see their own words written down, grasping that writing codes sounds, and breaking speech into words, syllables and sounds. These are the roots of later writing.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Produire de premiers écrits — Produire des écrits
clusters.summary: Your young child is making their very first attempts at writing, tracing letters, using letter names to represent the sounds they hear, drawing on models around the room, and seeing a short piece through from idea to revision. Playful, purposeful early writing builds real confidence.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your young child is learning to talk in different ways, recounting what they did, reciting a rhyme, joining a conversation, making a guess, and giving an absent adult the details they would need to understand. Using speech for varied purposes stretches their language and their thinking.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to write real texts, marking sentences with capitals and punctuation, encoding words and short sentences, using drafts to gather ideas, and building up to a coherent text of several lines for a genuine reader. Following a process from planning to revision is the key habit here.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is writing a variety of texts very regularly, telling stories, explaining, describing and giving justified opinions, while using a draft to plan and applying the conventions of written French. Frequent, varied writing is what builds a confident writer.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Recourir à l'écriture pour réfléchir et pour apprendre
clusters.summary: Your child is using writing as a tool to think and learn, listing and organizing ideas, jotting notes while listening, framing answers using the words of the text, and writing to explain a method or reason through a problem. Writing to learn makes their thinking clearer and more durable.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Regarder le monde, inventer des mondes
clusters.summary: Your teenager is reading works that both picture the real world and invent new ones, realist and fantastic fiction, poetry, the city, humanity's bond with nature, and science and progress. It invites them to reflect on society, the future and our shared responsibilities.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning a method for writing varied texts, calling up prepared language and ideas, organizing them into coherent paragraphs, drawing on the features of each genre, and using classroom aids and their language knowledge. It turns writing from a blank-page struggle into a structured process.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to revise their writing, rereading carefully, even aloud, to catch missing words, muddles and repetitions, checking spelling, and using class-built aids to fix and improve it. Learning that good writing is rewritten is a valuable early lesson.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: S'exprimer de façon maîtrisée en s'adressant à un auditoire
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to speak in a controlled, formal way to an audience, giving reports and presentations, telling a story engagingly, and sharing an opinion on a work, speaking continuously for several minutes. It is a real step up from casual conversation.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is reading works about identity and self-discovery, travel and the pull of elsewhere, love in poetry, and autobiographical writing. Meeting these themes helps them reflect on who they are and on how people come to understand themselves.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Se repérer dans la phrase simple
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to find their way around a simple sentence, spotting where sentences begin and end, recognizing statements, questions and commands, distinguishing the main word classes, and playing with sentences by moving or swapping parts. Understanding sentence structure supports both reading and correct writing.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is mastering the shared vocabulary for talking about language: the terms for sentence and clause structure, for verbs and their tenses and moods, for word classes and functions, and for how words are formed. Having precise names for how language works makes grammar and analysis far easier to discuss.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Vivre en société, participer à la société
clusters.summary: Your teenager is reading works that explore living together, conflicting values, relationships with others, the quest for independence, and satire that criticizes society. These texts invite them to think about their place among others and the tensions of social life.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Écouter et comprendre différentes formes d'écrits
clusters.summary: Your young child is learning that different kinds of writing serve different purposes, noticing the labels, lists, posters and books around the classroom, seeing that each one has a reader, and getting a feel for the special language of written texts read aloud daily. It is an early sense of what writing is for.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to listen actively in order to understand, following spoken messages and instructions, answering questions on what they heard, drawing on background knowledge, and noticing when they have not understood. Focused listening is the foundation for learning across the school day.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is deepening their listening, picking out and remembering key information, recognizing different kinds of spoken texts, responding personally and emotionally to what they hear, and keeping a questioning ear rather than accepting everything at face value. Attentive, critical listening supports learning in every subject.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Écrire pour réfléchir, apprendre et mémoriser
clusters.summary: Your child is using writing to think, learn and remember, reformulating a lesson in their own words, sorting and comparing information, summarizing, and writing short pieces to practise new grammar and vocabulary. Writing this way helps knowledge take root.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Écrire à la main de manière fluide et efficace
clusters.summary: Your child is making handwriting fast and effortless, automating their cursive, copying longer texts neatly and accurately, and also learning to type and use word processing to draft and lay out texts. Fluent handwriting and keyboard skills free their attention for what they want to say.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to write coherent texts using a real method, training the handwriting gesture, writing every day, working in different genres, drawing on their language knowledge, and rereading to improve. Across these early years they gradually write longer pieces on their own.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your child is treating writing as a full process, drafting and revising rather than writing in one go, growing their spelling vigilance, tailoring a one-to-two-page text to its reader, and using writing to think and learn. Regular, varied writing builds a capable, independent writer.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.summary: Your teenager is writing with more craft and purpose, inventing texts within a studied genre, planning and reworking rather than writing in a single pass, drawing on their reading to enrich their own work, and putting a reasoned judgment or a personal response into writing. They are becoming a more deliberate, expressive writer.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Élaborer une interprétation de textes littéraires
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to interpret literature, sensing the effects of literary language, knowing the main features of the different genres, placing a work in its era and movement, and using simple analytical tools. It is the start of reading not just for the story but for how and why it is written.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Établir des relations entre les mots
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring how words relate to one another, building word collections by theme and family, sorting them into categories, finding synonyms and antonyms, spotting prefixes and suffixes, and noticing different registers of language. Seeing these connections makes vocabulary richer and easier to remember.

clusters.subject: Français
clusters.domain: Établir des relations entre les mots
clusters.summary: Your child is deepening the links between words, connecting them through shared roots and word families, exploring synonyms, antonyms and words with several meanings, building and breaking apart derived and compound words, and becoming aware of where words come from. Understanding these relationships expands and organizes their vocabulary.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.domain: Clés de lecture du monde contemporain
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to read today's world through a few big ideas: how globalization ties distant places together while exposing deep inequalities, how great changes constantly reshape where activity happens, and how the push toward more sustainable ways of living is transforming societies. These lenses give them a grown-up framework for making sense of the news and the forces shaping the planet they will inherit.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.domain: Des territoires inégalement intégrés dans la mondialisation
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering why some places thrive at the heart of the global economy while others are left on the sidelines. Through real examples like the Roissy airport hub and New York's decision-making centers, they see how airports, industrial clusters and command centers plug regions into worldwide exchange, and how planning choices try to steer that unequal integration.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.summary: This is where your child builds the practical skills of a geographer: comparing places, questioning maps and data instead of taking them at face value, and looking at the same issue at local, national and global scales at once. They also learn to draw a 'croquis', a legend-based sketch map that turns their analysis of a territory into a clear visual argument.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.domain: La France et ses régions dans l'Union européenne et dans la mondialisation
clusters.summary: Your child is examining France's place in Europe and the wider world, from its cultural pull and uneven attractiveness to landmark sites like Disneyland Paris and the Kourou space center in Guiana. They learn to spot the main axes and hubs that organize French territory, seeing their own country as one player among many in a connected world.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.domain: La diversification des espaces et des acteurs de la production
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how and where things get made today, from Europe's aerospace industry to the giant port of Rotterdam. They follow global value chains that split the making of a single product across many countries, and see how trends like coastal concentration and rising trade flows keep reshaping the map of production.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.domain: La métropolisation : un processus mondial différencié
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring why people, money and power increasingly concentrate in big cities, comparing a world city like London with a French metropolis like Lyon as it transforms. They pick up tools such as the centre-periphery model to understand the unequal relationships between dominant cities and the spaces that depend on them.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.domain: Les espaces ruraux : une multifonctionnalité toujours plus marquée
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering that the countryside is no longer just about farming: rural areas now host tourism, housing and many other roles at once. Through examples like farm tourism and the spread of housing around cities, they learn how French rural spaces are being reshaped and sometimes split into very different zones.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.domain: Mers et océans : au cœur de la mondialisation
clusters.summary: Your child is learning why the seas are central to today's economy, carrying most world trade and even the undersea cables that make the internet work. They explore strategic passages like the Strait of Malacca, the tug-of-war over who controls the oceans, and France's own maritime strength thanks to its vast sea territory around the globe.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.domain: Notions et vocabulaire à maîtriser à l’issue de la classe de seconde
clusters.summary: Your child is building the core vocabulary that underpins all their geography work, from key ideas like 'territoire' and 'mondialisation' to the words for development, population, environment, risk and human movement. Mastering these precise terms lets them describe the world clearly and reason about it with confidence.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.domain: Thème 1 : Sociétés et environnements : des équilibres fragiles
clusters.summary: Your child is looking at how human societies and their environments hold together in a delicate balance, studying fragile places like the Amazon, the Alps and the Arctic. Through cases of climate change, natural hazards and pressure on water and land, they learn that whether a place is safe or vulnerable depends as much on human choices as on nature itself.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.domain: Thème 2 : Territoires, populations et développement : quels défis ?
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring how population and development shape countries very differently, from aging Japan to fast-rising Brazil, India and Russia. They come to see development as far more than economic growth, and to understand the twin challenges of huge or shrinking populations and of inequalities that persist both between countries and within them, including France.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.summary: Your child is studying a world constantly on the move, following tourists, students, workers and migrants across borders. From Dubai's magnetism to Mediterranean migration routes and Erasmus exchanges within Europe, they learn what drives people to travel and relocate, and the challenges this movement creates for the places involved, including France.

clusters.subject: Géographie
clusters.domain: Thème 4 : L’Afrique australe : un espace en profonde mutation
clusters.summary: Your child is taking a close look at Southern Africa as a region in the middle of deep change, where people move in complex ways and environments are both prized and under strain. Studying countries at very different stages of development, they see how a whole part of the world balances growth, migration and care for its natural resources.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.summary: Beyond the content, your teenager is building the working habits of a young researcher: questioning where information comes from, weighing conflicting viewpoints, and stepping back to examine their own reasoning. They also practise finding reliable sources, writing clear reading notes, speaking to defend an argument out loud, and managing their own work, so that university's lighter supervision feels less daunting.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.domain: Classe de première : « Acquérir des clefs de compréhension du monde contemporain »
clusters.summary: This first-year strand gives your teenager a few big keys for making sense of today's world: how the news is produced and can be shaped, how states and religions relate to one another, and why borders are drawn and disputed. The aim is to help them read current events with more depth and less naivety.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.summary: At the start of the course your teenager learns what each of its four lenses actually does: geography's ideas of space and territory, geopolitics as the study of power struggles over land, history's careful work with traces and archives, and political science's questions about how power and decisions are organised. It sets up the toolkit they'll use all year.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.domain: Thème 1 : Comprendre un régime politique : la démocratie
clusters.summary: Your teenager explores what democracy really means, from ancient Athens to today's European Union, and what separates it from authoritarian rule. Through cases and thinkers like Tocqueville, they see both its strengths and how it can weaken or collapse, which helps them understand the fragility of the freedoms they may take for granted.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.domain: Thème 1 – De nouveaux espaces de conquête
clusters.summary: Here your teenager looks at space and the oceans as the newest arenas where countries compete and cooperate, from the space race and naval power to shared projects like the International Space Station. Using China as a closing case, they see how nations extend their ambitions far beyond their own land.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.domain: Thème 2 : Analyser les dynamiques des puissances internationales
clusters.summary: Your teenager learns what makes a country powerful, not just armies and money but also language, culture, and tech giants, and why powers rise and decline over time. With examples like the United States and Russia, they grasp today's debates about whether nations should act alone or work together.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.domain: Thème 2 – Faire la guerre, faire la paix : formes de conflits et modes de résolution
clusters.summary: This strand helps your teenager understand why wars happen and how peace is built, from classic ideas like Clausewitz's view of war as politics by other means to modern terrorism and asymmetric conflicts. Studying today's conflicts and cases like the Middle East, they see both the logic of violence and the hard work of peacemaking through treaties and institutions.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.domain: Thème 3 : Étudier les divisions politiques du monde : les frontières
clusters.summary: Your teenager examines why states draw borders and what follows from those choices, from the German-Polish frontier to Europe's Schengen area and the law of the sea. They come to see borders not as simple lines on a map but as things negotiated, contested, opened, and closed, which shapes how people and countries live together.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.summary: Your teenager learns the difference between history, a careful reconstruction of the past, and memory, how groups remember and feel it. Through the genocides of the twentieth century, the Algerian War, and courts from Nuremberg to Rwanda's local gacaca, they see how societies seek justice and come to terms with painful pasts, a demanding but important subject at this age.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.domain: Thème 4 : S'informer : un regard critique sur les sources et modes de communication
clusters.summary: In a world flooded with news, your teenager learns to look critically at where information comes from and how it can inform, persuade, or manipulate. From the Dreyfus affair and wartime propaganda to conspiracy theories online and whistleblowers, they build the judgment to tell trustworthy sources from unreliable ones, a skill that matters well beyond the classroom.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.domain: Thème 4 – Identifier, protéger et valoriser le patrimoine : enjeux géopolitiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager discovers that heritage, from Venice and the Parthenon Marbles to old mining sites and UNESCO listings, is never neutral: it can be a source of pride, tourism, conflict, or diplomacy. They learn how societies decide what to protect and why, and how the very idea of heritage keeps widening.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.domain: Thème 5 : Analyser les relations entre États et religions
clusters.summary: Your teenager studies how political power and religion have intertwined across centuries and places, from medieval popes and caliphs to secular Turkey, India, and the United States today. They learn that religion still shapes politics worldwide and that separating the two, laïcité included, is an uneven and ongoing story.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.domain: Thème 5 – L'environnement, entre exploitation et protection : un enjeu planétaire
clusters.summary: Here your teenager examines the long relationship between societies and their environment, from the Neolithic and Industrial revolutions to today's climate negotiations at the COP conferences. They come to see the environment as something humans have both exploited and protected, and climate change as a major geopolitical challenge of their generation.

clusters.subject: HGGSP
clusters.summary: Your teenager explores knowledge itself as a source of power: how it is produced by scientific communities, spread through literacy and schooling, and fought over by states, from Cold War spying to today's cyberspace and cyberdefense. They grasp why controlling information and expertise has become a central stake in our knowledge-driven world.

clusters.subject: HLP
clusters.domain: Découverte du monde et pluralité des cultures
clusters.summary: Your teenager looks at how the Renaissance and the age of exploration turned the old picture of the world upside down: new continents, new peoples, and a cosmos that suddenly felt open and even infinite. Along the way they see how encountering very different cultures, and the violence of religious wars, pushed Europeans to question their own certainties. It teaches them that discovery is not just about new places but about learning to see yourself differently.

clusters.subject: HLP
clusters.summary: Your teenager explores the many ways people have tried to capture and represent the world, from the invention of perspective in painting to atlases, encyclopedias, and the spread of the printed book. They think about how imagination and even fiction help us understand nature and ourselves, not just cold facts. The aim is to see how art, science, and literature each offer their own picture of reality.

clusters.subject: HLP
clusters.summary: Your teenager studies the art of speaking well: how eloquence works in courtrooms, assemblies, and public ceremonies, and what makes a spoken argument persuasive. They compare what speech and writing each do best, and learn the classic building blocks of a well-made discourse. It gives them tools to express themselves clearly and to notice when someone is trying to sway them.

clusters.subject: HLP
clusters.summary: Your teenager digs into a big question with a long history: what really separates humans from animals? From Montaigne to the naturalists, they weigh debates about animal intelligence, the famous "animal-machine" quarrel, and what our treatment of animals says about the place we give ourselves in nature. It connects directly to today's live questions about animal rights and how we should treat other living beings.

clusters.subject: HLP
clusters.summary: Your teenager uses literature and philosophy to ask what "being human" means in an age of powerful new technology, digital networks, and the promise of an "augmented" human. They wrestle with real dilemmas like how to balance using nature against protecting it. It invites them to think carefully about where human capabilities, and human responsibilities, should stop.

clusters.subject: HLP
clusters.summary: Your teenager reflects on one of the questions they are living every day: who am I, really? They examine how well we can ever know ourselves, how much our identity is shaped by other people's eyes, and whether the "self" is one stable thing or something that keeps changing. At an age of intense self-discovery, it gives them serious ideas to make sense of their own inner life.

clusters.subject: HLP
clusters.domain: Semestre 1 : les pouvoirs de la parole
clusters.summary: Your teenager learns how words move people, to persuade, to please, to move, and sometimes to seduce, and practices this power both in writing and out loud. They learn to spot and analyze the tricks of rhetoric in a text or speech, and then to build convincing arguments of their own. It sharpens both their critical ear and their own voice.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to see themselves as part of a longer story, shared with France, Europe and the wider world, and to use that awareness to think for themselves. Rather than just memorising the past, they practise stepping back from today's headlines to understand them, weighing the choices and constraints people faced in their own time. The goal is a young citizen who can reason about the world with perspective and make up their own mind.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning how historians slice up the past into named stretches of time such as centuries, ages and eras, and what each of those labels really means. They discover that these divisions are not obvious facts but choices people made, and that the way we cut up history has itself changed over the centuries. This gives them a mental map of the big periods and a healthy sense that even 'when things happened' is something to think about.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Maîtriser et utiliser des repères chronologiques et spatiaux
clusters.summary: Your teenager is building a solid framework of key dates, people and places, and learning to place events in their proper time and setting. They practise connecting facts across different eras and regions, zooming in and out from a local to a global view, and asking what changed and what stayed the same. These habits turn scattered facts into a coherent picture and help them make sense of the world around them in daily life.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: S’approprier les exigences, les notions et les outils de la démarche historique et de la démarche géographique
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to work like a real historian or geographer: asking a genuine question, forming and testing ideas, and building an argument backed by evidence. They read documents such as maps, images and statistics with a critical eye, ask where our knowledge of the past actually comes from, and learn to spot reliable sources from doubtful ones. They also produce their own sketches and maps and use the specialised vocabulary of each subject accurately.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 1 : L'Europe bouleversée par la Révolution française (1789-1815)
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying how the French Revolution swept away the old order and gave birth to a bold new idea: a nation of equal citizens, with rights declared for all. They follow the fall of the monarchy, the first Republic born amid war, and how Napoleon then dominated much of Europe while carrying some of the Revolution's principles with him. This period shows how quickly a whole society can be remade, and where many of today's ideas about rights and nationhood began.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 1 : Le monde méditerranéen : empreintes de l'Antiquité et du Moyen Âge
clusters.summary: Your teenager is exploring the Mediterranean as a birthplace of Europe, from Athenian democracy and the Roman Empire to the moment Rome became Christian. They see how goods, people and ideas moved constantly across this sea, and how Jewish, Christian and Muslim worlds met, traded and sometimes clashed. It helps them understand where many of Europe's political ideas, faiths and cultural roots come from.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 1 – Fragilités des démocraties, totalitarismes et Seconde Guerre mondiale (1929-1945)
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning how the 1929 economic crash shook the world and how fragile democracies responded very differently, some with reform and others by sliding into dictatorship. They study the fascist, Nazi and Stalinist regimes, the descent into the Second World War, and its most extreme horrors, including the genocides of the Jews and the Roma. This difficult chapter helps them understand how democracies can fail and why defending them matters.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 1 – Totalitarismes et Seconde Guerre mondiale
clusters.summary: Your teenager is looking closely at France during the Second World War: the Vichy regime, the German occupation and collaboration, set against the courage of the Resistance and de Gaulle's Free France. They also study how the war on the Eastern Front intensified the genocide of Europe's Jews. It is a hard but essential story about occupation, choices under pressure, and how ordinary people resisted or complied.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 2 : Les transformations politiques et sociales de la France de 1848 à 1870
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying a France in rapid change, from the 1848 revolution that introduced universal male suffrage to the authoritarian Second Empire that industrialised the country with railways and factories. They connect these political shifts to deep economic and social upheaval, seen through examples like the Le Creusot ironworks and figures like Victor Hugo. It shows how democracy, industry and society advanced together, unevenly and often in tension.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 2 : XVe-XVIe siècles : un nouveau rapport au monde, un temps de mutation intellectuelle
clusters.summary: Your teenager is discovering how the 15th and 16th centuries opened Europe to the wider world, as trade shifted from the Mediterranean to the Atlantic and Europeans built colonial empires. They learn about the great voyages and their harsh consequences for the peoples of the Americas, alongside the intellectual awakening of the Renaissance, humanism, the printing press and the Reformations. This period marks the start of a connected world and a new way of thinking, with both its brilliance and its brutality.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 2 – Du monde bipolaire au monde multipolaire
clusters.summary: Your teenager is tracing how the world reorganised itself after 1945, split into two rival blocs during the Cold War, then reshaped again after 1991 into a world of many competing powers. Along the way they study decolonisation and the rise of newly independent nations, the building of Europe, and even the space race as a symbol of superpower rivalry. This gives them the backbone for understanding the geopolitics of the world they live in today.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 2 – La multiplication des acteurs internationaux dans un monde bipolaire (de 1945 au début des années 1970)
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning how, after 1945, a shattered world tried to build a new order through the UN and war-crimes trials, even as the Cold War divided it in two. They see new players step onto the stage, from Mao's China to newly independent states, and study tense moments like the Cuban missile crisis and the wars in Indochina and Vietnam. Closer to home, they follow France's own upheavals, including how the Algerian crisis brought a new Republic. It shows a world becoming more crowded and complex.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 3 - La France de 1945 à nos jours : une démocratie
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying how France rebuilt itself as a modern democracy after 1945, from the postwar reforms to the founding of today's Fifth Republic. They follow the end of the colonial empire and the painful Algerian War, the winning of women's legal equality including the right to vote and to abortion, and how immigration and education reshaped society. This is the story of the France they live in and the rights they now take for granted.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 3 - Les remises en cause économiques, politiques et sociales des années 1970 à 1991
clusters.summary: Your teenager is exploring the turbulent years from the 1970s to 1991, when the oil shocks ended postwar prosperity and a new, more market-driven economy took hold under leaders like Reagan. In France they study reforms such as the abolition of the death penalty and the changes of the Mitterrand years, while abroad the Soviet bloc collapses and Southern Europe turns democratic. Together these show a world being remade economically, politically and culturally in living memory.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 3 : L'État à l'époque moderne : France et Angleterre
clusters.summary: Your teenager is comparing two paths of modern statehood: France building a powerful absolute monarchy around the king at Versailles, and England limiting royal power and asserting the rights of Parliament. They see how these models fed the ideas of the Enlightenment and inspired the founding of the United States, along with the glaring limits of those new ideals for enslaved and Native peoples. It lays the groundwork for understanding where modern democracy and the rule of law come from.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 3 : La Troisième République : un régime, un empire colonial
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning how the Third Republic finally gave France a lasting democratic regime, with free public schooling, guaranteed liberties and the 1905 separation of church and state that established laïcité. At the same time they confront its contradictions: the divisive Dreyfus affair and the antisemitism it exposed, and the vast colonial empire built on domination, seen up close through a city like Algiers. It helps them weigh the ideals and the failings of a republic that still shapes France.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 4 - Le monde, l'Europe et la France depuis les années 1990, entre coopérations et conflits
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying the world they were born into, from the multipolar rivalries and new forms of conflict such as terrorism and 9/11, to attempts at global cooperation through international justice and the environment. They follow the deepening of the European Union and the euro, and in France the reaffirming of laïcité and new rights around equality. This chapter connects recent history directly to the news and debates around them today.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 4 : Dynamiques et ruptures dans les sociétés des XVIIe et XVIIIe siècles
clusters.summary: Your teenager is exploring the society of 17th and 18th century Europe, still organised into rigid orders of nobles, clergy and commoners, yet already stirring with change. They study the rise of scientific thinking from Galileo to the Encyclopédie, the growth of ports fuelled by the plantation economy and slave trade, the hardships of peasant life, and the first steps of the industrial revolution. It reveals a world caught between old hierarchies and powerful new ideas about to overturn them.

clusters.subject: Histoire
clusters.domain: Thème 4 : La Première Guerre mondiale et la fin des empires européens
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning how the First World War became a long, industrial and truly global conflict that mobilised entire economies and colonial empires, with the Battle of the Somme showing its staggering human cost. They study its extreme violence against civilians, including the Armenian genocide, and how the peace treaties redrew the map of Europe and dissolved its old multinational empires. It helps them understand a catastrophe that reshaped the modern world.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.domain: 1. Arts et société à l'époque antique et au haut Moyen Âge
clusters.summary: Your child explores the earliest chapters of art history, from Greek and Roman temples and statues to the way ancient cities slowly turned into medieval ones. They learn how founding myths and the human body were pictured in art, giving them a first sense of where our visual culture comes from.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your child discovers how art travelled and mixed across the medieval world, from cathedrals and castles to the hand-painted illuminated manuscripts that were the books of the age. They also meet the lively debate over whether religious images should even exist, seeing that art has always stirred strong opinions.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.domain: 3. Le sacre de l'artiste (XIVe-début XVIIe s.)
clusters.summary: Your child follows the moment when artists became celebrated figures, backed by wealthy patrons in the Italian city-states, and when the printing press first let images and texts be copied for everyone. They see how painting, music and theatre flowered across Europe as ideas circulated between countries.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.domain: 4. État, société et modes de vie (XIIIe-XVIIIe s.)
clusters.summary: Your child learns how art reflected the way people actually lived, from the furniture and decoration of their homes to how they danced and pictured nature. They see the arts and the sciences advancing side by side, and how different kinds of painting came to be ranked as more or less important.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.domain: 5. L'art au temps des Lumières et des révolutions (1750-1850)
clusters.summary: Your child studies art in an age of upheaval, when paintings carried political messages and, for the first time, a wide public and professional critics began judging what they saw. They explore the pull between belief in progress and a longing for the past, and the growing importance of raw feeling and emotion.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.domain: 6. De la Belle Époque aux « années folles » : l'ère des avant-gardes (1870-1930)
clusters.summary: Your child enters the bold, experimental art of the modern city, alive with jazz, circus and music-hall, and reshaped by the arrival of photography, film and recorded sound. They see artists turning away from realistic scenes toward inner, imagined worlds while also searching for roots in a fast-changing world.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.domain: 7. Les arts entre liberté et propagande (1910-1945)
clusters.summary: Your child examines how art could be used both to challenge power and to serve propaganda in a turbulent, war-torn era. They discover the birth of abstract art, the way electricity dazzled artists, and how women artists won new freedom to create.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.domain: 8. Les arts à l'ère de la consommation de masse (de 1945 à nos jours)
clusters.summary: Your child looks at art from 1945 to today, shaped by a global, consumer world where cultures blend and design fills everyday life. They explore how artists respond to big contemporary questions such as energy and the climate, working in styles that range from realistic to abstract.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.domain: Analyser et décrire une œuvre d'art
clusters.summary: Your child learns to look and listen carefully, then put into words what an artwork is made of, its materials, colours, shapes and sounds, using the right vocabulary. This early skill of describing before judging helps them move from simply seeing a work to understanding what it means.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager examines the tangled relationship between art and those in power, and how governments shape cultural life through their policies. They also learn to read the choices behind a city's design and its buildings, seeing how art and architecture are woven into society.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: By the end of this stage, your child can recognise and place a set of landmark artworks from Antiquity to today, and compare works to draw thoughtful connections between eras and cultures. They can also speak personally about art they have made or experienced, showing their own engagement rather than just repeating facts.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager builds the tools to place any artwork in its time and place and to talk about it precisely, using the specific vocabulary of painting, music, cinema and more. They learn to compare works, spot what genuinely connects or separates them, and understand today's art by tracing it back through history.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager develops a mature, critical eye, learning to analyse artworks in depth and to cross-check what they feel against reliable information. They also come to value the heritage right around them and connect it to the wider world's masterpieces.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your child learns to describe an artwork accurately, date it to the era and culture that made it, and offer a simple reasoned opinion of their own. They practise presenting art out loud and sharing accounts of museum visits or meetings with heritage professionals.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager learns to approach visual and musical culture thoughtfully and to express an argued, well-supported point of view, in writing and out loud. Through regular visits to museums and heritage sites, they open their sensitivity to art and learn to let reasoning and emotion work together rather than against each other.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.domain: Donner un avis argumenté sur une œuvre
clusters.summary: Your child learns to say what they think about an artwork and back it up with a first look at what it shows, building a vocabulary for both emotions and the ways we talk about art. They meet the great myths and founding stories that so many artworks draw on, helping them read the figures and scenes in front of them.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager gathers the cultural, historical and geographical landmarks needed to situate any artwork and to speak about it spontaneously and personally. They learn to visit museums and heritage sites on their own, and to bring reasoned analysis and genuine feeling together when facing a work.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager takes part in a shared creative project, helping design an exhibition, write its labels, or build digital pieces about art. They practise debating artworks and even guiding visitors, turning what they have learned into something they present to a real audience.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager contributes to producing a real or virtual exhibition, from its concept and layout to the wall labels, and creates imaginative digital pieces about art and heritage. They also learn to defend a position in a debate and to engage seriously with the views of others.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.domain: Se repérer dans un lieu d'art
clusters.summary: Your child learns how a museum works, how to find their way using maps and signs, and how to behave in a place devoted to art. They also become aware that heritage is fragile and needs protecting, and discover the people whose jobs keep it safe.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager works directly with real artworks, describing carefully what they see or hear before interpreting it, and studying at least one original piece in front of the work itself rather than a copy. Visiting an actual building to study its architecture teaches them how much a first-hand encounter differs from a picture in a book.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager learns why standing before a real work matters and how it differs from any reproduction or recording. They study art through the concrete conditions of its making, including who commissioned it and how it reached the world, and visit a building in person to understand architecture on site.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.domain: Situer une œuvre dans son contexte
clusters.summary: Your child builds a personal 'imaginary museum' of the works they have studied and learns to place each one in its period and part of the world. They read the little identity label beside a work, connect a story to its many artistic versions, and link art to the everyday lives of the people who made it.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager studies great art centres in depth, each rooted in a place and time, ranging from Classical Greece, Florence and Byzantium to Angkor, Han China, Al-Andalus and modern New York, Berlin and Dakar. Focusing closely on these hubs shows how a particular city or region can concentrate an extraordinary burst of creativity.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager explores the bigger questions around art itself: the materials and techniques behind it, how works travel and are exchanged, and the museums and institutions that house them. They also look at the art market and money, and how critics, patrons and the public shape a work's reputation over time.

clusters.subject: Histoire des arts
clusters.summary: Your teenager learns to build a structured, well-argued case about an artwork, gathering varied sources and weighing them for reliability. They practise forming a genuine aesthetic judgement backed by reasons, and developing a full project such as a report or website to present their work in art history.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Des espaces transformés par la mondialisation
clusters.summary: Your child is looking at how a connected global economy reshapes real places, from a major African region to the shipping lanes of the world's seas and oceans and the territory of the United States. It gives them a concrete sense that trade, resources and power flows leave visible marks on the map, and helps them see their own life as part of a wider, interconnected world.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Dynamiques territoriales de la France contemporaine
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring how France itself is changing, from busy city regions to quiet rural and mountain areas and the farms, factories and services that keep the country running. Working with familiar national geography helps them understand where people live and work, and why some places thrive while others struggle.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Histoire - CM1 - Le temps des rois
clusters.summary: Your child is meeting the famous kings who shaped France, from Louis IX and François I to Henri IV and Louis XIV at Versailles. Through these vivid characters and stories they begin to grasp how power, religion and the arts worked in earlier centuries, and start building a timeline of their country's past.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Histoire - Sixième - L'empire romain dans le monde antique
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering the Roman Empire, how it conquered and united many peoples, spread its way of life, and connected with distant lands along the old Silk Road. Learning how such a vast ancient world worked gives them an early foundation for understanding empires, cultures and how ideas travel between them.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Histoire - Sixième - Récits fondateurs, croyances et citoyenneté dans la Méditerranée antique
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring the ancient Mediterranean through the founding stories of Rome, the Greek city-states, and the birth of Jewish monotheism. These myths and early beliefs help them see how communities explained their origins and organised life together, laying the groundwork for ideas about citizenship and religion.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Le XVIIIe siècle. Expansions, Lumières et révolutions
clusters.summary: Your child is studying the century that overturned the old order, from the new ideas of the Enlightenment to the French Revolution and Napoleon's Empire. They also confront harder truths of the era, including the Atlantic slave trade that fuelled merchant wealth. It helps them understand where modern ideas of rights, freedom and equality come from.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how today's world took shape after 1945, through the Cold War divide, the independence of former colonies, the building of Europe, and the conflicts that followed the fall of the Berlin Wall. Connecting recent history to the headlines of today helps them make sense of the world they are growing up in.
