clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: L’Europe, un théâtre majeur des guerres totales (1914-1945)
clusters.summary: Your child is studying the two World Wars and the turbulent years between them, including the rise of dictatorships, the occupation of France, and the genocide of the Second World War. These are difficult but essential subjects that help them understand how democracy can be lost and why remembering these events matters.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Maîtriser et utiliser des repères chronologiques et spatiaux
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to think like a historian and geographer: placing events and places in context, connecting facts across time and space, and zooming between local and global scales to understand a situation. These reasoning skills help them organise knowledge and judge what really matters, in the classroom and in the wider world.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Pratiquer différents langages en histoire et en géographie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to express what they know in different ways, by drawing simple maps and diagrams, reading photographs and maps, using the right vocabulary, and speaking and writing clearly. Practising these skills helps them organise their thinking and share ideas about history and geography with confidence.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Pratiquer différents langages en histoire et en géographie
clusters.summary: Your child is building on earlier skills to communicate about history and geography with more independence, producing their own maps, diagrams and slideshows, using precise vocabulary, and writing structured narratives and arguments. This teaches them to present evidence and reasoning clearly rather than simply repeating facts.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: S'informer dans le monde du numérique
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to find and judge information online, checking where a fact comes from, deciding whether it can be trusted, and choosing the right tools and sources for a question. In a world full of digital content, these habits are essential for becoming a careful, critical user of information.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Se repérer dans l'espace : construire des repères géographiques
clusters.summary: Your child is building a mental map of the world, learning to name and locate key places, describe different kinds of spaces, and understand how a place can be viewed from the local level all the way up to the global. These landmarks give them a stable frame for making sense of everything they study in geography.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Se repérer dans le temps : construire des repères historiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to organise the past in their mind, putting events in order, placing them in the right period, and reading timelines ("frises chronologiques"). Memorising a few key dates and landmarks gives them a scaffold so that new history lessons have somewhere to fit.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Société, culture et politique dans la France du XIXe siècle
clusters.summary: Your child is studying 19th-century France as it slowly became a republic, following the long fight to win the right to vote and the place of women in a changing society. It shows them that the democratic rights we take for granted today were hard-won and relatively recent.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Société, Église et pouvoir politique dans l’occident féodal (XIe-XVe siècles)
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring medieval Europe, where lords ruled the countryside, towns and trade were growing, and kings gradually built up their power. Understanding this world of castles, peasants and rising cities helps them see how modern societies and states slowly took shape.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: S’approprier les exigences, les notions et les outils de la démarche historique et de la démarche géographique
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to work like a real historian or geographer, forming hypotheses, questioning documents rather than trusting them blindly, judging which sources are reliable, and building reasoned arguments backed by maps and evidence. These are demanding intellectual habits that carry far beyond the subject into how they weigh any claim.

clusters.subject: Histoire-géographie
clusters.domain: Transformations de l'Europe et ouverture sur le monde aux XVIe et XVIIe siècles
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering the age of the Renaissance and the great religious upheavals, when humanism renewed art and learning, the Reformation split Christianity, rival empires clashed, and French kings moved toward absolute power. It helps them understand the roots of both the culture and the conflicts that shaped modern Europe.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is reading how ancient Greeks and Romans wrote about love and desire, from tender poems and letters to stories of seduction, heartbreak and betrayal. They meet the gods of love, Venus and Cupid, in literature and art, and learn that the ancients had several different words for love. It is a way to see that the emotions of teenage life have been explored and named for thousands of years.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering how ancient people tried to make sense of the world before modern science, from early ideas about the human body and medicine to guesses about the stars, the shape of the earth and natural disasters. They also see how myths were a way of explaining why things are the way they are. This helps them appreciate how human curiosity and the search for answers have grown over time.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring how ancient thinkers began to reason and question the world, learning from teachers and their students in the first schools of philosophy. They look at how belief, magic and early science lived side by side, and compare the many gods of Greece and Rome with the rise of religions with a single god. It is a chance to think about the difference between believing, knowing and doubting.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is looking at how the ancient world invented, built and reshaped things, from the real machines of engineers like Archimedes to imagined automata and early dreams of robots. They see how people farmed and designed landscapes to tame nature, and meet mythical makers such as Prometheus and Daedalus. It connects the human urge to create and build with stories that are still told today.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.domain: Justice des dieux, justice des hommes
clusters.summary: Your child is thinking about right and wrong through ancient stories of crime, guilt and punishment, asking who is really to blame when things go wrong. They weigh the tension between what is fair and what those in power demand, and learn the ancient warning against going too far, called hubris. These are timeless questions about justice that still shape how we think about fairness today.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring how the ancient world saw the bond between people and animals, as partners, rivals and sometimes near-equals, and asking what really sets a human apart. They meet monsters and hybrid creatures that mix human and animal shapes, and see how the ancients catalogued animals both real and imaginary. It opens up a big question about what it means to be human.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is reading ancient stories about where the world came from and how fate shapes a life, meeting cursed heroes and doomed families whose destiny plays out in tragic theatre. They see how oracles, prophecies and dreams were thought to carry warnings from fate. These powerful stories invite reflection on how much of life we can control and how much is simply given to us.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.domain: La cité entre réalités et utopies
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how ancient cities were planned and governed, from the first city-planners to the different systems of rule such as democracy and tyranny, and the old question of what makes a good leader. They also explore imagined perfect societies, from Atlantis to modern dreams and warnings about the future. It links the ancient world directly to how we think about living together and running a country.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is meeting the great wise figures of antiquity, such as Socrates and Seneca, and the practical advice they gave about how to live well, find happiness and face death. They think about whether one should take part in public life or step back from it. At an age full of big questions, it offers time-tested ideas about what a good and meaningful life looks like.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is following how the peoples around the ancient Mediterranean expanded, conquered and traded, and how they borrowed ideas and customs from one another across the sea. They look at the cycles of war and peace that shaped the region. It helps them see the Mediterranean as an early meeting point of cultures, a theme that still echoes in the world today.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering the great cities and archaeological sites of the ancient Mediterranean, the libraries and schools where knowledge lived, and the art that modern artists still imitate. They trace how these ancient places and works have survived and continue to influence us. It shows that the classical world is not gone but still present in the buildings, ideas and images around us.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring how the ancients imagined the edges of the known world and told stories of great sea voyages and heroic journeys full of trials. They learn the ancient value of hospitality toward strangers and exiles, and see the Mediterranean as a shared sea uniting many peoples. It combines adventure with early ideas about welcoming those who come from far away.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is building the groundwork for reading the ancient world: placing key events on a timeline, learning where things happened on a map, and getting to know the famous figures of myth and history. They also compare ancient works with modern ones and study a few Greek and Latin key words that carry deep cultural meaning. These reference points make everything else they study easier to place and understand.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is deepening the tools for studying antiquity: setting ancient works next to modern ones to see how they are echoed and reworked, mapping the ancient world, and following a clear historical timeline. They also study Greek and Latin key words whose meaning is both linguistic and cultural, and revisit the great emblematic figures that recur across eras. It gives them a confident framework for reading the classical world.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring how the ancient world saw the self and the stranger, from ghosts and apparitions of the dead to the line drawn between Greeks, Romans and 'barbarians', citizens and slaves. They also look at what it meant to speak another people's language, and at ancient ideas of a divided self. These themes open up thoughtful conversations about identity, belonging and how we treat those who are different.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is reaching an advanced stage of ancient Greek grammar, working through tricky verb forms like the aorist and contract verbs, the subtle role of moods and aspect, and clauses that express purpose or result. They also master irregular comparatives, the third declension and the many uses of the participle. This careful study of how Greek is built lets them read real ancient texts more independently.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is strengthening their command of ancient Greek through verb systems such as the future, the middle-passive and the optative, along with questions, relative pronouns and reflexives. They learn some of the language's more idiomatic turns, like special uses of common words and turning phrases into nouns with the article. Each new pattern gives them another key to unlock the meaning of original Greek writing.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.summary: Your child is tackling the finer points of Latin grammar, from special case constructions for place, time, quality and age to reported speech, questions and the subjunctive in various clauses. They also learn irregular, impersonal and defective verbs and the more unusual pronouns and number words. This advanced work equips them to read authentic Latin authors with real understanding.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.domain: Étude de la langue — Grec — Morphologie nominale
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how Greek nouns, adjectives and pronouns change their endings, including the demanding third declension and common irregular adjectives. They also practise forming comparatives and superlatives and making words agree correctly in a sentence. Mastering these building blocks is the foundation that lets them start reading and translating ancient Greek.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.domain: Étude de la langue — Grec — Morphologie verbale
clusters.summary: Your child is learning the core Greek verb tenses, including the present, the imperfect and the aorist, in their different voices, plus the present participle and a set of very common verbs. Getting these conjugations under their belt is essential, because the verb is the heart of every Greek sentence. It is a key step toward reading real texts on their own.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.domain: Étude de la langue — Grec — Syntaxe de la phrase
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how Greek sentences fit together, from clauses that give a cause or a time to infinitive and participial constructions and the two kinds of negation. They also study how the tenses convey different shades of an action. Understanding this sentence structure is what turns a string of words into a meaning they can actually translate.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.domain: Étude de la langue — Grec — Syntaxe nominale
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how Greek uses cases and small words to express relationships, such as pointing out 'this house', showing place with the right prepositions, and the many meanings carried by the genitive and dative. These are the connective tissue of the language. Getting comfortable with them helps sentences click into place when they read.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.domain: Étude de la langue — Latin — Morphologie nominale
clusters.summary: Your child is getting an overview of the five Latin noun declensions and learning to spot which one a word belongs to, along with the common demonstrative and relative pronouns. These changing word-endings are the backbone of Latin. Recognising them is the first real skill needed to begin reading and translating the language.

clusters.subject: LCA latin / grec
clusters.domain: Étude de la langue — Latin — Syntaxe de la phrase
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how Latin sentences are structured, from the useful ablative absolute and relative clauses to infinitive constructions, clauses of time and cause, and the main uses of the subjunctive for orders and wishes. They also see how the tenses shape meaning. This grasp of sentence-building is what lets them move from single words to translating a whole passage.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.summary: Your teenager is looking at how artists use their work to take a stand, bear witness, or provoke, and at how ideas get argued out in everything from reasoned discussion to heated exchange. They are building the habit of thinking freely and critically while respecting other people's views, and learning to tell a verifiable fact from a belief or an opinion. It is the kind of grounding that helps them read the world around them with a sharper, fairer eye.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.summary: In their Spanish studies, your teenager is exploring how different peoples, faiths, and cultures have lived side by side across the Spanish-speaking world, from the shared coexistence ("convivencia") of medieval Spain to the encounters and conquests of the Americas. They also look at moments of tension and exclusion alongside moments of joy shared through festivity, music, and sport. It gives them a richer, more human sense of how societies handle difference.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.summary: Through Italian culture, your teenager is seeing how art and comedy have been used to hold up a mirror to society, expose its cruelties, and push for change. They study feminist and protest movements and the bold artistic revolutions of Futurism and Neorealism that shaped 20th-century Europe. The thread running through it all is how creativity becomes a form of resistance.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.summary: Your teenager is exploring how artists and writers turn their own emotions and inner life into creative work, and how they stage themselves within it as a genuine artistic gesture rather than mere self-display. A key focus is the coming-of-age novel, the story of a young person growing up and finding their place. At an age when they are figuring out who they are, these works speak directly to their own experience.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.summary: In their Italian studies, your teenager is examining how thinkers, writers, and artists relate to those in power, whether they serve it, quietly question it, or openly defy it. They also look at how language itself, and the place of religion alongside the state, have been sources of power throughout Italian history. It helps them see that influence is not only political but also cultural and spiritual.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.domain: Préambule spécifique à l'enseignement de spécialité d'espagnol
clusters.summary: This part of the Spanish specialty gives your teenager the tools to work like a real analyst: reading and commenting on all kinds of documents, from press articles to infographics and broadcasts, and weighing what they mean. They compare Spanish-speaking cultures with others, connect literature to the society that produced it, and deepen their grasp of the Hispanic world's place globally. These are skills they will carry well beyond the language itself.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.summary: Your teenager is discovering the many ways Italian culture has reshaped reality, from the rhetoric of exaggeration and the invention of perspective and cinema to tales of the strange and the supernatural. They also explore how dreams, madness, and the unconscious run through Italian literature and art. It is an invitation to notice that a picture of the world is always a way of interpreting it.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.domain: Thématique Circulation des hommes et circulation des idées
clusters.summary: In the Spanish-speaking world, your teenager is tracing how people, knowledge, and cultural practices move and get passed on, through travel, exile, and the ties between generations. They look at how these cultures write their history and hold onto personal memory. It helps them understand that identities and ideas are shaped by movement and exchange rather than staying fixed in one place.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.summary: Your teenager is exploring the power of imagination across several cultures, from the Italian pursuit of beauty and the German notion of the uncanny ("das Unheimliche") to the utopias and dystopias of English literature. They see how the mind invents extraordinary worlds, gives shape to our deepest fears, and reflects on what it means to be human. Studying these imagined worlds sharpens how they think about the real one.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.summary: Through Italian history and culture, your teenager is looking at how power is embodied, seized, and challenged, and at what it means to be an active, engaged citizen rather than a passive one. They study the long tradition of disobedience and resistance and the symbolic authority held by leaders, clergy, scholars, and artists. It gives them a thoughtful lens on the balance between authority and the individual conscience.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.summary: Your teenager is exploring how meeting others shapes who we are, whether the encounter brings connection or confrontation with difference. They look at love and friendship and their darker sides of absence and loneliness, and at how individuals fit, or clash, with the groups around them. The core idea is that we build ourselves through our encounters with the world beyond us.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.domain: Thématique Représentations et expressions de la mémoire
clusters.summary: In their German studies, your teenager is examining how a society remembers its past, through the German notion of a "culture of remembrance" (Erinnerungskultur) and the duty of memory. They look at how history and place carry collective and personal memory, and at how that memory is documented, imagined, or retold in literature. It is a meaningful way to think about how societies come to terms with difficult history.

clusters.subject: LLCER
clusters.summary: Your teenager is exploring how the English-speaking world was shaped by voyages, exploration, and the movement of people, and how migration and exile sit at the heart of its culture. They also look at the pull of roots and heritage, and how belonging to a place helps build who we are. It offers a nuanced picture of identity in a world defined by both journeys and homelands.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.domain: Compréhension de l'oral et de l'écrit
clusters.summary: In their third foreign language, your child is learning to genuinely follow real material: stories and novels, news broadcasts, films, and opinion pieces. They pick up the main points, use clues like context, cognate words, images and tone to work out what they don't yet know, and start to tell facts apart from an author's viewpoint or irony. This is the stage where a language stops being isolated words and becomes something they can actually read and listen to for meaning.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to speak and write on their own in the third language, from introducing themselves and describing everyday life to giving a prepared talk, telling a story, and expressing feelings, wishes and opinions with reasons. They build fluency by leaning on phrases they've mastered, work around missing words, and organise their ideas with connectors so a listener or reader can follow easily. The goal is confident, structured expression rather than a few memorised sentences.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.summary: Here your child learns to hold a real conversation: asking and answering questions, agreeing or disagreeing with nuance, being polite, and keeping an exchange going even when they hit a gap. They also practise mediation, passing on the key points of a text or discussion to someone else and explaining cultural references so people understand one another. These are the practical social skills that let a language work between people, not just on paper.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.domain: Lexique en lien avec les axes culturels
clusters.summary: Your child is building the vocabulary needed to discuss the grown-up themes at the heart of the course, such as the arts, migration and solidarity, science and ethics, media and information, memory and heritage, and the digital world of AI and privacy. This is deliberately mature, opinion-rich language that lets them talk and write about real issues in the third language rather than staying at the level of everyday small talk.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.domain: Outils linguistiques – LVC : Lexique en lien avec les axes culturels
clusters.summary: At this earlier stage your child gathers the words tied to the year's cultural themes: family and generations, the environment and consumption, myths and legends, the past and heritage, cooperation and conflict, and describing oneself and one's style. Having this vocabulary ready means they can actually engage with each topic in the language instead of getting stuck for words.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.domain: Outils linguistiques – LVC – Lexique en lien avec les axes culturels
clusters.summary: Your child learns targeted sets of words that help them build an argument and discuss ideas, such as advantages and drawbacks, communication and sharing, creation and celebration, difference and inclusion, discovery and risk, and scientific progress. This is the vocabulary that lets them weigh pros and cons and hold a real discussion on the course's themes in the third language.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.domain: Outils linguistiques – LVC – Phonologie et prosodie
clusters.summary: Your child works on how the language actually sounds: pronouncing the sounds that don't exist in French, and using stress, rhythm and intonation to make their meaning clear and expressive. The aim is speech that stays intelligible and natural enough to support real, flowing conversation.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.domain: Repères culturels – LVA, LVB et LVC
clusters.summary: Beyond the language itself, your child explores the culture behind it through big themes like art and power, diversity and inclusion, people and nature, science and responsibility, and how public and private life are drawn. It's a chance to understand how another society thinks and lives, which deepens both their curiosity and their grasp of the language.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.summary: At this stage your child begins to explore the culture of the language through study themes such as change and transition, revisiting myths and classic works, relations between generations, a close look at one region, self-representation, and how the past lives on in the present. These themes give real substance to the language and connect it to how people actually live and remember.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.domain: Repères linguistiques – LVC : Compréhension de l'oral et de l'écrit
clusters.summary: Early on, your child learns simple strategies for making sense of what they hear and read: leaning on familiar and transparent words, and using a document's title, layout and images to guess what it's about before diving in. At this beginner level the goal is to understand short, concrete texts on familiar topics, which builds the confidence to tackle harder material later.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.domain: Repères linguistiques – LVC : Expression orale et écrite
clusters.summary: Your child takes their first steps in producing the language: introducing themselves, describing familiar people and places, expressing tastes and wishes, retelling a short story, and situating things in time and space. They also pick up practical habits like copying, taking dictation and drafting to build up their speaking and writing bit by bit.

clusters.subject: LVC (3e langue)
clusters.domain: Repères linguistiques – LVC : Interaction orale et écrite, médiation
clusters.summary: At the beginner stage your child learns to manage simple exchanges: greeting and thanking politely, asking and answering everyday questions, saying they agree or disagree, asking for help, and passing on basic facts like a time, place or price. They also learn to keep going when stuck by using gestures and accepting pauses, so that even limited language becomes usable in real situations.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is starting to tune their ear to a new language, following a very short story read aloud with the help of pictures and understanding simple spoken instructions from the teacher. They also learn to name the letters of the alphabet and spell familiar words out loud. This early listening is where confidence with the sounds of another language begins.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is connecting the language classroom to the wider world, learning to notice how people in other countries live, celebrate and see things differently. They begin to reflect on their own culture too, handle real or virtual experiences of travel and exchange, and talk about what those encounters felt like, even when an exchange did not go smoothly. It helps them become curious, open-minded travellers rather than just language learners.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: In their first year of lycée, your teenager explores a foreign culture around the big theme of living well together, touching on art, work, sport, the environment, city life, generations and the weight of history. The point is not just to communicate but to understand the culture, imagination and history that a language carries. This broadens how they see the world and their place in it.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: In the final years of lycée, your teenager studies a foreign culture through rich themes such as identities and exchanges, private and public life, art and power, citizenship, diversity, science and memory. They learn to compare how different societies are built and keep transforming, and to discuss real issues with nuance in the target language. This prepares them to think and argue as an informed citizen across cultures.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning the building blocks of the language's grammar: how nouns, articles and pronouns work, how to say things in the past, present and future, and how to link ideas into fuller sentences. These are the nuts and bolts that let them move from single words to expressing real, connected thoughts.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning what it really means to hold a two-way conversation in a foreign language, building meaning together with a partner rather than just speaking or listening on their own. They practise picking up on what the other person says, keeping the exchange going and handling realistic situations where they genuinely need to communicate. This is the skill that makes a language usable in real life.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning the words closest to their own everyday life in the new language: family and friends, the body and clothes, feelings and tastes, daily routines, and the days and seasons. Starting with familiar things gives them something concrete to talk about and makes the language feel useful from the very first lessons.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering the world around the new language through its animals, food, homes, festivals and famous tales and characters. They also start to recognise a few landmarks of the countries where the language is spoken, such as flags, currencies and typical landscapes. It opens a window onto another culture in a way that feels playful and age-appropriate.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is picking up the vocabulary of school and daily life in the new language, including numbers, classroom activities and routines, sports and hobbies, and words for the weather. These are the everyday terms they will use again and again, so the language quickly becomes part of ordinary classroom life.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is expanding their vocabulary into meatier subjects such as the environment, media and social networks, school and work, travel and migration, the arts, and cultural landmarks of the countries studied. This wider word bank lets them read real documents and discuss topics that matter to teenagers, rather than staying with only the basics.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to read a range of texts in the foreign language, from short simple pieces toward longer ones, following a story from start to finish and understanding personal messages and practical notices. They also build strategies for guessing meaning from clues instead of stopping at every unknown word. Reading independently opens up huge amounts of new language and content.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is beginning to read short, simple texts in the new language, such as messages, recipes, stories and poems, and to pull out the main idea or a key detail. They lean on pictures, context and familiar words to make sense of what they read. This early reading turns the language from something only heard into something they can decode on the page.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your teenager is refining their vocabulary so they can choose the most precise, fitting word rather than just any word that works. They learn words in real contexts and build the specific language needed to describe, explain, tell a story and argue a point. This precision is what lifts their expression from getting by to sounding genuinely fluent.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to speak on their own for a short stretch, using memorised phrases to talk about themselves and their world, make a simple announcement, or read a short text aloud with expression. They start from set models and gradually add their own words. Speaking without a back-and-forth partner builds the confidence to hold the floor in another language.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is developing longer, more independent speaking, able to describe people and experiences, tell a short story, explain a cultural fact and give and defend an opinion with reasons. They also learn to work around gaps in their vocabulary, correct themselves and speak with clearer pronunciation and the right tone. This turns halting phrases into genuine, connected talk.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is taking their first steps in real conversation, greeting others, introducing themselves, asking how someone is and answering simple questions on familiar topics. These short polite exchanges follow ritual patterns they can reuse. It gives them the very first taste of actually talking with someone in the new language.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning what it means to express themselves in the foreign language through both speaking and writing, moving from simply rephrasing a source toward genuinely creative expression. They also pick up strategies to keep going when words are missing, by paraphrasing, adapting the message or making things clearer. This is where they take ownership of the language as their own voice.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to keep a simple conversation going, greeting people, asking and answering questions, and exchanging practical information such as a time, a price or directions. They practise starting, holding and ending short everyday exchanges. This back-and-forth is what makes the language feel alive and social rather than a solo exercise.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is handling richer conversations, asking for and giving information, expressing feelings and opinions, reacting to what others say and rephrasing things for someone who did not understand. They also learn to report the key points of a document and read body language and gestures. These skills let them cope with real, spontaneous exchanges, including while travelling.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to understand spoken and written language in stages, starting from the easy-to-grasp parts such as the title and general theme and working toward more complex meaning. They build strategies like forming hypotheses from clues and the type of document. Learning how to make sense of harder material is what lets them tackle authentic texts and recordings with confidence.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to speak alone for a few moments in the new language, describing themselves and their home, reciting a rhyme, song or poem from memory, and retelling a short story with the help of pictures. They also read a short prepared text aloud with good sounds and rhythm. These first solo performances build early confidence and a feel for pronunciation.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to use a text to get something done, finding and sorting the information that answers a question, following written instructions or a recipe, and spotting whether something is allowed or forbidden. This puts reading to practical use and shows that understanding a text can lead straight to action.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.domain: À l'articulation des activités langagières : la médiation
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning mediation, the skill of making something they have read or heard understandable to someone else who could not access it on their own. This includes explaining cultural references, taking notes and summarising for classmates, translating or interpreting, and helping a group work together. It positions them as a bridge between people and languages, a genuinely useful real-world skill.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.domain: À l'articulation des activités langagières : la médiation
clusters.summary: In the final years, your teenager deepens mediation, passing content from one person or language to another by summarising a talk or a set of documents, translating, interpreting or dubbing, and facilitating group and intercultural work. Being able to relay and explain meaning to others is a mature communication skill that goes well beyond the classroom.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to listen to short, slow, clear messages in the new language and pick out the topic and key points. They tune in to the specific sounds, rhythm and intonation of the language and use audio cues and pictures to guess unfamiliar words. Careful listening is the foundation that all the other language skills build on.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to follow more demanding listening, from familiar everyday expressions to longer talks, discussions and even debates on topics they know. They work with varied audio and video, using clues to grasp the main ideas and pick out specific information, often to carry out a task. This is what lets them keep up with real speech at natural length.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is starting to write in the new language, copying words and writing them from dictation, building simple sentences from a model, and producing a short letter, message or little story linked with basic connectors. Beginning to put the language on paper themselves is a big step from only speaking and listening.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.summary: Your child is developing real writing skills, from linking sentences with connectors and filling in a form to summarising a document, taking notes and writing creative stories, poems and articles. They learn to write about themselves and past experiences and to reply to messages and letters. Being able to write clearly lets them express fuller, more organised ideas in the language.

clusters.subject: Langues vivantes
clusters.domain: Écrire pour décrire, informer ou exprimer un point de vue
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to write for a purpose in the new language, filling in a form, writing about themselves, jotting down a notice or instructions, and posting simple comments online while reacting to others. This shows early on that writing is a practical tool for describing, informing and sharing an opinion, not just an exercise.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.domain: Fonctions : continuité, dérivabilité, limites, représentation graphique
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to read a function the way a scientist reads a graph: where it climbs and where it dips, where it settles down and where it shoots off toward infinity. They use the derivative to map out these behaviours and get comfortable with the exponential and its mirror-image, the natural logarithm. It is the toolkit that lets them predict how a quantity changes, which underpins almost everything they will meet in economics, physics or biology.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.summary: Your teenager is discovering integration, which is really just a precise way of measuring the area under a curve, and through it the average value of something that keeps changing. They learn to compute these areas exactly and also to estimate them with clever approximation tricks, even a method that throws random darts to guess an area. This matters because so many real quantities, from total distance travelled to accumulated cost, are areas in disguise.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to reason about experiments with clear-cut outcomes, like a coin landing heads or a treatment working or not. They study how to count the ways things can happen and predict, on average, how a repeated trial will turn out. These probability models are the foundation of everything from quality testing to opinion polls, and they give your child a rigorous way to think about chance rather than relying on gut feeling.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.summary: Here your teenager moves from counting outcomes to quantities that vary continuously, like a waiting time or a measurement that can take any value. They learn to read a probability as an area under a curve and to work out the average and spread of such quantities. This is exactly how real-world randomness, from radioactive decay to how long a bus takes to arrive, is modelled, and it prepares them for statistics in almost any field.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to describe how things grow, shrink or settle over time, whether it is money in an account, a population of animals, or a cup of coffee cooling down. They see how the same mathematical ideas capture savings, radioactive decay and predator-prey cycles alike. It is a powerful lesson that a handful of tools can model wildly different real-world situations, sharpening their ability to make predictions.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.domain: Modèles définis par une fonction d'une variable
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to turn a real situation, such as minimising a cost or maximising an area, into a function they can study. Using the derivative, they find the best possible outcome, the sweet spot where something is at its highest or lowest. This is optimisation, one of the most practical uses of mathematics, and it teaches your child to translate a messy problem into a question maths can actually answer.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to run differentiation in reverse, recovering a quantity when only its rate of change is known. They also meet their first differential equations, the language physics uses to describe motion, cooling and growth. Being able to move from a rate back to the quantity itself is a genuine leap in mathematical maturity and opens the door to modelling how systems evolve.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.summary: Your teenager is using maths to measure something socially charged: how unequally wealth or income is shared across a population. They learn to summarise a spread of values and to build tools like the Gini index and Lorenz curve that put a single number on inequality. It is a striking example of how mathematics informs public debate, letting your child compare countries or track a trend with real rigour rather than opinion.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning why a poll of a few thousand people can tell us something reliable about millions. They explore how results fluctuate from sample to sample, how to build a confidence range around an estimate, and how to test whether observed data really fits a theory. With a bit of Python simulation, they see these ideas come alive, gaining the statistical literacy to judge the surveys and studies they meet everywhere in the news.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to spot and measure a relationship between two quantities, like study time and grades, by plotting the data and fitting a best line through it. They then use that line to make careful estimates, while learning the limits of stretching a trend too far beyond the data. This is the core skill behind data analysis and forecasting, and it teaches a healthy caution about reading too much into a correlation.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.summary: Your teenager is working with sequences, step-by-step number patterns that model things measured at regular intervals, such as a yearly balance or a monthly population. They learn to describe where a sequence is heading in the long run and to translate real situations into these step models, sometimes computing them on a calculator or computer. It builds the patient, iterative thinking that underlies both mathematics and programming.

clusters.subject: Maths complémentaires
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning the precise language of mathematical reasoning: what makes a statement true or false, how to negate it, and how a single well-chosen counter-example can topple a general claim. They meet the vocabulary of sets and the 'for all' and 'there exists' quantifiers that give maths its exactness. Beyond maths, this trains clear, careful thinking, the ability to say exactly what one means and to test whether an argument really holds up.

clusters.subject: Maths expertes
clusters.summary: Your child is digging into the hidden structure of whole numbers: how they divide, share common factors, and behave when you look only at remainders. Along the way they meet the classic results that make this work, and they see why it all matters through real applications like the check digits on barcodes and the RSA system that keeps online messages secret.

clusters.subject: Maths expertes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to turn real situations into networks of points and connections, and to describe them with grids of numbers called matrices that can be added, multiplied and raised to powers. This lets them model things that evolve step by step, from populations to random movement, and even understand the kind of maths behind how a search engine ranks web pages.

clusters.subject: Maths expertes
clusters.domain: Nombres complexes : point de vue algébrique
clusters.summary: Your child is being introduced to a new kind of number that extends the ordinary ones and makes previously unsolvable equations solvable. They learn to add, multiply and manipulate these numbers with confidence, building the comfortable fluency they will rely on for everything that comes next.

clusters.subject: Maths expertes
clusters.domain: Nombres complexes : point de vue géométrique
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering that these new numbers can be pictured as points in the plane, so that calculations turn into distances and angles they can actually see. This bridge between algebra and geometry gives them a powerful second way of thinking, and even opens a door onto the beautiful, endlessly detailed shapes that come from repeating a simple rule.

clusters.subject: Maths expertes
clusters.summary: Your child is connecting these numbers to the sine and cosine functions they already know, which gives a surprisingly clean way to handle angles and rotations. They learn a few elegant formulas that make otherwise messy trigonometric calculations simple, and they practise choosing whichever way of writing a number makes a problem easiest.

clusters.subject: Maths expertes
clusters.domain: Utilisation des nombres complexes en géométrie
clusters.summary: Your child is putting these numbers to work as a tool for solving geometry problems, proving that points line up or meet at right angles and measuring lengths and angles cleanly. They explore the elegant symmetry of points spread evenly around a circle, which ties back to constructing regular shapes and even underpins techniques used in modern signal processing.

clusters.subject: Maths expertes
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to solve equations more completely by allowing these new numbers as answers, so that even equations with no ordinary solution can be fully cracked. They pick up practical techniques for breaking a polynomial down once one solution is known, and they see the deep links between an equation's coefficients and its roots.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: (Se) repérer et (se) déplacer en utilisant des repères et des représentations
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to find their way and move through space using landmarks, and to describe positions with precise words like left, right, in front and behind. They also record a route as a set of coded steps and use those to guide a robot or an on-screen character, an early taste of giving clear instructions.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is discovering how computer programs work: a program is a precise list of steps, and it uses stored values, loops, and 'if' choices to get things done. They also see how an action can be triggered by a click or a key press, building the logic behind the apps and games they use every day.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is beginning to think like a mathematician by spotting patterns and rules: continuing a growing sequence, finding a missing number that keeps an equation true, and following step-by-step calculation recipes. Standing in for an unknown number with a letter is their very first step toward the algebra they will meet later.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to make sense of real datasets by studying two characteristics of a group at once in a cross-table, and by filtering people who match a condition using AND, OR and NOT. They use a spreadsheet to turn numbers into clear tables and charts, a practical skill for work and everyday decisions.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is getting to know angles: naming them (right, acute, obtuse and more), knowing that a right angle is 90 degrees and a full turn is 360, and seeing how angles relate when lines cross. This gives them the vocabulary and reference points they need to describe shapes and measure turns.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Apprendre des procédures de calcul mental
clusters.summary: Your child is building clever mental-maths tricks so they can calculate quickly without writing anything down, such as rounding a number to the nearest ten and adjusting, or reaching the next ten. They also learn to multiply by doubling and by breaking numbers apart, which makes bigger sums feel manageable.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Apprendre des procédures de calcul mental
clusters.summary: Your child is sharpening their mental-calculation strategies with larger and trickier numbers, including doubling and halving to multiply or divide by 4 and 8 and working with tens, hundreds and thousands. They begin doing the same with simple decimals, gaining speed and confidence for everyday calculations.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is making core calculation skills automatic: doing arithmetic in their head with decimals, fractions and percentages, switching a number between those forms, and solving simple equations. They also learn to estimate the size of an answer first, so they can catch mistakes and judge whether a result makes sense.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying integration, the mathematics of measuring the area under a curve and the average value of a changing quantity. They learn to compute these areas exactly and by approximation, and to give them meaning in real contexts like physics or economics, one of the pillars of advanced maths and science.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to work with letters that stand for numbers: expanding, factoring and simplifying expressions, and turning a word problem into an equation they can solve. This algebra lets them describe general rules and prove why something is always true, a big step up in mathematical thinking.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is extending their mental arithmetic with handy shortcuts, such as using the distributive property to break a multiplication apart, recalling multiples of 25 and 50, and multiplying or dividing by 100 and by 0.1. These strategies help them calculate flexibly and check their written work.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning the scalar product, a tool that links the lengths of two vectors and the angle between them. They use it to prove that lines are perpendicular, to find angles and distances, and to establish classic geometry results, connecting algebra and geometry in a powerful way.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Calculer avec des grandeurs mesurables ; exprimer les résultats dans les unités adaptées
clusters.summary: Your teenager is calculating real-world quantities like areas and volumes of common shapes, and combined measures such as speed and density. They also convert between units and check that their answers are expressed sensibly, skills that matter whenever maths meets everyday life or science.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning what a circle really is: all the points the same distance from a centre, with a disc being everything inside. They pick up the vocabulary of radius, diameter and chord and use distance ideas to solve simple problems, building a precise picture of round shapes.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to count the number of ways things can be arranged or chosen without listing them all, using multiplication and addition principles, permutations and combinations. These counting tools underpin probability and appear in many real problems, from passwords to lottery odds.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Comparer, estimer, mesurer des longueurs, des masses, des contenances, des durées
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to compare and measure everyday quantities: how long, how heavy, how much a container holds, and how much time passes. They use real instruments like rulers, scales and measuring jugs, read graduated scales, and estimate sizes, connecting numbers to the physical world around them.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is deepening their study of how functions change, including the idea of a curve that bends upward or downward (convexity) and where it switches. They use these tools to sketch functions accurately and to prove inequalities, refining the calculus that supports higher study in science and economics.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Comprendre et utiliser des nombres entiers pour dénombrer, ordonner, repérer, comparer
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to count, compare and order whole numbers, using symbols for equal, less than and greater than. By grouping objects into tens, hundreds and thousands they grasp how our number system is built, and they connect a number's position in a list to how many come before it.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Comprendre et utiliser la notion de fonction
clusters.summary: Your teenager is meeting the idea of a function, a machine that turns each input into an output. They learn to read functions from formulas, tables and graphs, to move between these forms, and to use straight-line (linear and affine) functions to model everyday situations like proportional pricing.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Comprendre et utiliser les notions de divisibilité et de nombres premiers
clusters.summary: Your teenager is exploring how whole numbers break apart: multiples and divisors, quick divisibility tests, and prime numbers as the building blocks of all numbers. These ideas let them simplify fractions and solve practical puzzles, and they lie at the heart of modern topics like data encryption.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is developing the habits of a young mathematician: searching for solutions by trying things out, reasoning and checking whether answers make sense, and explaining their thinking to others. They also learn to represent problems with drawings and symbols and to model real situations with maths.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning the law of large numbers: the more data you gather, the closer averages settle around a true value. Through simulations and inequalities they see how sample size affects precision, the reasoning behind opinion polls and reliable estimates from real-world data.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Continuité des fonctions d'une variable réelle
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying continuity, the idea that a function's graph can be drawn without lifting the pen, and the powerful result that guarantees a solution exists between two values. They use step-by-step methods like bisection and Newton's method to pin down where an equation is solved.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning how quantities that keep multiplying grow, through geometric sequences and exponential functions. They model real cases such as compound interest or a spreading process and solve when a threshold is crossed, understanding why exponential growth accelerates so dramatically.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying steady, constant growth through arithmetic sequences and straight-line (affine) functions. They connect the rate of change to the slope of a line and use these models to describe and predict situations that increase or decrease by the same amount each step.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning about distance in geometry: finding the midpoint of a segment, carrying and comparing lengths with a compass, and the simple truth that the shortest path between two points is a straight line. These ideas ground careful drawing and reasoning about shapes.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your young child is discovering what numbers are for: telling how many, remembering exact quantities, and comparing collections. Through fingers, counting rhymes and hands-on play they learn that a quantity stays the same however you arrange it, and that each number is one more than the one before, laying the very first foundations of counting.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Découvrir les nombres et leurs utilisations
clusters.summary: Your young child is learning to count objects reliably, to know that the last number said tells how many there are in all, and to compare which group has more or fewer. They start adding and taking away small amounts and using the number strip to move forward and back, playful first steps into arithmetic.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning the derivative, which measures how fast a quantity is changing at any instant, seen as the slope of a curve, a speed, or a marginal cost. They compute derivatives of standard functions and find the tangent line, one of the most useful ideas in all of maths and science.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is using the derivative to understand how a function rises and falls: the sign of the derivative tells its direction, and this reveals maximums and minimums. They summarise all this in a variation table and apply it to solve real optimisation problems, like finding a best or cheapest option.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Effectuer des opérations sur les fractions
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to calculate with fractions: multiplying a fraction by a whole number, and adding and subtracting them, including when the denominators differ. They also solve and invent their own fraction word problems, turning an abstract idea into something they can use.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to recognise, name and construct plane figures and solids, to draw perpendicular and parallel lines with proper tools, and to understand reflection symmetry. They also read maps, follow and write construction steps, and program simple movements, blending hands-on drawing with spatial reasoning.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is moving into more formal geometry: the angles of a triangle, the Pythagorean theorem, Thales' theorem, and trigonometry in right triangles. They learn how transformations like reflections and rotations act on figures and begin building proper mathematical proofs from properties of shapes.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Espace et géométrie — La géométrie plane
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to build symmetric figures on grid paper and to know the properties of familiar shapes, such as which sides are equal or parallel. They practise using the ruler, set square and compass correctly, gaining the precision that good geometry drawing requires.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to recognise and name basic flat shapes like squares, rectangles, triangles and circles, and to draw them accurately given their sides and right angles. Using a compass to draw a circle from its centre is one of their first real geometry constructions.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Espace et géométrie — tracés et instruments
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to use simple geometry tools with care: spotting and drawing straight alignments with a ruler, checking right angles with a set square, and transferring lengths or finding a midpoint. These are the basic hand skills behind all accurate drawing.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Explorer des grandeurs : la longueur, la masse
clusters.summary: Your young child is exploring how long and how heavy things are by comparing objects directly, lining them up or weighing them on a balance. They pick up the everyday words for longer, shorter, heavier and lighter, and begin to reason that if one thing is lighter than another, order follows.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Explorer les solides et les formes planes
clusters.summary: Your young child is handling cubes, balls, pyramids and flat shapes, turning and fitting them to learn how they look and feel. They begin to name shapes with real words and to understand that a shape stays the same whatever its colour, size or position, while sorting them builds early logical thinking.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is meeting the exponential function, the unique function that equals its own rate of change, and the special number e. They learn its algebraic rules and use it to model growth and decay, from money earning interest to radioactive materials fading away.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning the natural logarithm, the function that undoes the exponential and turns multiplication into addition. They study its properties and curve and use logarithms alongside exponentials to solve equations, a tool that appears everywhere from acidity scales to sound and earthquake measurements.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying the sine and cosine functions, the smooth waves that repeat forever and describe anything cyclical. They learn their derivatives and graphs and solve trigonometric equations, mathematics essential for physics, sound, light and any recurring phenomenon.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is extending trigonometry from triangles to the whole number line using the trigonometric circle and the radian. They explore how sine and cosine repeat and mirror themselves, and even revisit how Archimedes approximated pi, connecting angles, circles and periodic motion.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to measure and calculate perimeters, areas, volumes, angles and durations, and to choose and convert the right units. They use tools like the protractor and apply formulas for common shapes, solving everyday problems that mix numbers with real measurements.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is doing geometry with coordinates: writing the equation of a circle, describing a line by its normal vector, and finding where curves and lines meet. This 'geometry on a grid' lets them solve shape problems with algebra, the approach behind computer graphics and mapping.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is taking their first steps in computational thinking: writing and following ordered instructions, repeating steps with loops, and fixing errors in a faulty program. They program a character to follow a route and even run programs 'unplugged' on paper, learning the logic behind coding.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Interpréter, représenter et traiter des données
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to handle data: organising it, reading and drawing bar charts, pie charts and histograms, and computing counts and frequencies. They also calculate averages, medians and range to summarise a set of figures, essential skills for making sense of statistics in the news and beyond.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to reproduce and construct squares, rectangles, triangles and circles on grid or plain paper, and to use the ruler, set square and compass. They learn the marks that show right angles and equal lengths, and start completing figures to make them symmetric.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is constructing more complex figures from written instructions, checking symmetry by folding, and understanding parallel and perpendicular lines. They learn to justify what kind of figure they see by testing its properties, an early move from simply looking to genuine geometric reasoning.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to handle money: finding the total value of coins and notes, making up a given amount, comparing sums and working out change in pretend shopping. They also meet how euros and centimes relate and what the comma means in a price, connecting maths to real life.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering proportionality, the idea behind recipes, prices per kilo and scaling a figure up or down. They learn to spot when two quantities grow together, to solve problems using proportion tables and unit values, and to read scales on maps, a hugely practical everyday skill.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to solve word problems on their own, from single-step addition and multiplication to two-step problems and fair sharing. Drawing bar diagrams and number-line sketches helps them picture what a problem is really asking before they calculate.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is tackling richer word problems that may need several chained steps, multiplicative comparisons, or finding the best option under constraints. They learn to explain their strategy in words, diagrams and calculations, building the reasoning and persistence that maths rewards.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning reflection symmetry: what it means for two figures to mirror each other across an axis, and how to construct the reflection of a point or figure using a set square, ruler and compass. Understanding the perpendicular bisector makes these constructions precise.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is developing spatial vision by picturing assemblies of cubes from different angles: drawing their top, front and side views, reading flat drawings and nets, and matching views to the right 3D shape. This mental manipulation strengthens how they imagine objects in space.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is building fast, reliable mental arithmetic: memorising number facts and multiplication tables, halving even numbers, and adding or subtracting whole tens and hundreds. Regular timed practice helps these facts become automatic, freeing their attention for harder maths later.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to describe and follow movements through space, producing and reading a sequence of directions between a start and an end point. They trace routes on a grid or town map from written or coded instructions, using precise words like quarter-turn and about-turn.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Le repérage dans le temps et les durées
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to tell the time on a clock, from whole hours to halves and quarters, and to set the hands to a given time. They learn the hour and minute units, tell morning from afternoon, and start measuring how much time passes during the day.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Le repérage dans le temps et les durées
clusters.summary: Your child is reading clocks and digital displays confidently in hours, minutes and seconds, and learning how these units relate. They solve real problems about times of day and durations, using resources like timetables, an everyday skill they will use for life.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to see a fraction as the result of a division: a divided by b. They fill in missing factors in multiplication equalities and discover that a fraction can represent a whole number, a decimal, or a number that is neither, deepening what fractions really mean.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning about area, the amount of surface a shape covers, by counting squares on a grid and using units like the square centimetre. They discover that perimeter and area are different things, and start using formulas for squares, rectangles and triangles.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to recognise and compare angles, deciding whether one is right, acute or obtuse, and knowing that a right angle measures 90 degrees. They learn to name and notate angles and to build new ones by folding and adding, seeing that an angle's size does not depend on the length of its sides.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning about capacity, how much a container holds, by comparing, pouring and measuring with a graduated cup. They learn the units from millilitre to hectolitre and how they relate, and calculate by converting and adding capacities, a skill straight from the kitchen.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is consolidating clock reading and the links between time units, from seconds to centuries. They solve the two key kinds of duration problem, finding how long passed between two moments and finding a time from a start and a duration, including conversions that need careful handling.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering fractions as equal parts of a whole, learning the words half, quarter, numerator and denominator, and reading and writing simple fractions. They compare fractions, find equivalent ones, add and subtract them in simple cases, and use them to measure lengths, making a tricky idea concrete.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is deepening their work with fractions: comparing them, placing them on a number line, finding a fraction of a quantity, and switching between mixed numbers and improper fractions. They reuse multiples and times tables as tools, strengthening the fraction sense they will need in secondary school.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to use geometry instruments purposefully: the compass to draw circles and carry lengths, the ruler and paper strips to transfer distances, and the set square for right angles. They also grasp that an angle's size does not change when its sides are made longer.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to compare and measure lengths with a graduated ruler, to know the units from millimetre to kilometre, and to choose and use the right one. They estimate lengths, draw segments to a set size, and begin finding the perimeter around a shape.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is working with lengths across different units, converting to and from the metre, ordering them, and adding or subtracting mixed measures. They compute perimeters of rectangles and circles, learn that a circle's circumference is proportional to its diameter (the constant being pi), and use reference distances to estimate.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to compare how heavy objects are by hefting them or using a balance, and to find the heaviest or lightest. They learn the units gram, kilogram and tonne, how to choose the right one, and use reference masses of familiar objects to estimate.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is working with masses across units, converting to and from the gram, comparing and ordering them even when each is given differently, and adding or subtracting mixed measures. They also learn the metric prefixes and how successive mass units relate, connecting weighing to the number system.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering decimal numbers, the numbers with a comma, and how they link to fractions of ten and a hundred. They learn to read, write, compare and order them and to place them on a number line, opening up amounts like money and precise measurements.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is building a solid understanding of whole numbers: reading, writing and ordering them, and grasping place value, that a digit's worth depends on its position. They count by grouping into tens, hundreds and thousands and place numbers on a number line, extending their range as the years go on.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is extending whole numbers into the millions and billions, comparing and ordering them and mastering place value through grouping and exchanging. They also start exploring multiples and divisors, spotting multiples of 2, 5 and 10 from a number's digits, early number-theory ideas.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is deepening their command of whole and decimal numbers: place value, the ten-for-one relations between units, and multiplying or dividing by powers of ten. They learn to multiply decimals, do Euclidean division, and round results, seeing why multiplying by a number below one makes things smaller.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is meeting chance: recognising random experiments, listing all possible outcomes, and telling apart events that are impossible, certain or somewhere in between. They learn to express and calculate probabilities and to see that observed results settle near the theoretical value, the beginnings of statistical thinking.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning what addition, subtraction, multiplication and division really mean and mastering the written column methods for whole numbers. They pick up the proper vocabulary, meet ideas like even and odd numbers and commutativity, and see how division undoes multiplication.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is carrying out the written algorithms for the four operations, now including decimals and long multiplication, and Euclidean division with a one-digit divisor. They also learn to respect brackets and to estimate an answer beforehand to check that their calculation is plausible.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning about 3D solids like the cube, cuboid and pyramid, describing their faces, vertices and edges. They build these solids, including folding a flat net into a cube, connecting the flat shapes they know with the objects around them.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your child is building and describing solids such as cubes, cuboids, pyramids and prisms, knowing how many faces each has and of what kind. They link a solid to its flat drawings and nets and reason spatially about which squares of a net become opposite faces, sharpening their 3D imagination.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is studying limits, how a function behaves as its input grows very large or approaches a certain value, and the asymptotes that describe its curve far out. They learn a key result that the exponential outgrows any power, resolving tricky cases and describing a curve's long-term shape.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.domain: Manipulation des vecteurs, des droites et des plans de l'espace
clusters.summary: Your teenager is extending vectors into three dimensions to describe lines and planes in space and how they sit relative to one another. They learn to combine vectors and use bases and coordinates, the mathematics behind 3D modelling, engineering and physics.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning about the full set of real numbers: intervals, absolute value as distance, and the difference between rational numbers and irrationals like the square root of 2 and pi. They bracket numbers with decimals to any precision and round results sensibly, building a rigorous view of number.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning vectors in the plane, which capture direction and length and are linked to translations. They add vectors, work with their coordinates and norms, and use collinearity to test whether points are aligned, tools that bridge geometry and algebra.

clusters.subject: Mathématiques
