They practise researching and pulling together reliable information, producing clean visual and multimedia documents, and working as a team on shared online spaces. These communication skills matter because even the best engineering idea is useless if you cannot make others understand and believe in it.

clusters.subject: Sciences de l'ingénieur
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to test real products in the lab and on the computer: fitting sensors to measure how something performs, running experiments safely, and building simulations and 'digital twins' to predict behaviour before touching the real thing. They also check that data really travels correctly across a communication network. This hands-on measuring and modelling teaches them to base decisions on evidence rather than guesswork.

clusters.subject: Sciences de l'ingénieur
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to plan and run their own experiments, from setting up the measurement and network equipment to justifying every choice of instrument and procedure. They also run computer simulations, then compare the predicted results with real measurements and tune the settings step by step to make a product perform better. This loop of test, compare and improve is exactly how engineers refine designs in practice.

clusters.subject: Sciences de l'ingénieur
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how new ideas are actually invented: using creativity techniques to imagine original solutions, deciding when to gently improve an existing product versus reinvent it, and shaping designs around real users' needs and comfort. They turn ideas into virtual and physical prototypes with 3D modelling and rapid-prototyping tools, then test them and judge whether they are worth pursuing. This gives them a real feel for the full journey from a spark of an idea to a working, useful object.

clusters.subject: Sciences de l'ingénieur
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to describe how machines and circuits behave using the language of physics and mathematics: modelling motion, forces, electrical circuits and controlled systems with equations, then solving them to predict performance. They work with the core tools of mechanics and electronics, from friction and dynamics to feedback control and turning algorithms into working code. This is demanding, rigorous work that builds the mathematical backbone engineers rely on to design things that work the first time.

clusters.subject: Sciences de l'ingénieur
clusters.summary: Your child is learning the foundations of motion: how to set up reference frames to describe exactly where something is, how position turns into velocity and acceleration, and how forces cause a moving object to speed up or change direction. They also learn to write the geometric relationships that link the moving parts of a mechanism. These basics are the starting point for all the physics of moving machines they will study later.

clusters.subject: Sciences de l'ingénieur
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to analyse how solid parts move and push on each other within a machine, including how friction affects joints and how motions combine when parts are linked together. They use compact mathematical tools to represent both the movement and the forces transmitted through connections. This lets them understand real mechanisms, like gears and linkages, rather than just idealised points.

clusters.subject: Sciences de l'ingénieur
clusters.domain: Raisonner, pratiquer une démarche scientifique, expérimenter
clusters.summary: Your child is being introduced to the scientific method as it applies to engineering: asking a question, forming a hypothesis, designing and building an experiment, measuring carefully, and drawing conclusions. They handle real instruments, learn about measurement uncertainty, and start using simulations, spreadsheets and graphs to make sense of results. At this age it builds the habit of testing ideas against evidence instead of assumptions, a foundation for everything technical that follows.

clusters.subject: Sciences de l'ingénieur
clusters.summary: Your child is learning the fundamentals of electrical circuits: how idealised resistors, capacitors, inductors and switches behave, how direct and alternating current supplies differ, and how to describe signals that repeat over time. They apply the basic laws that govern currents and voltages to work out what is happening inside a circuit. This gives them the electrical grounding needed to understand the electronics inside almost every modern device.

clusters.subject: Sciences de l'ingénieur
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to follow energy through a system: calculating power and efficiency, drawing up an energy balance to track what comes in, goes out and is stored, and recognising energy in its different forms such as motion and position. Understanding where energy goes, and how much is wasted, is central to designing systems that are efficient and to making sense of today's questions around energy use.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning what food actually does inside the body: how it gets broken down along the digestive tract, how the blood carries nutrients to the organs, and why our needs change as we grow or become more active. They also explore where food comes from and how it is transformed and preserved, from bread and yoghurt made with living microorganisms to salting and refrigeration that keep food safe to eat. This helps them make sense of everyday meals and start building healthy eating habits.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.domain: Cycle de vie et reproduction des êtres vivants
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering how living things grow, reproduce, and pass through the stages of life, from a flowering plant to an animal to the human body. They learn the changes that happen at puberty, the organs involved in reproduction and what each one does, and how pollinators like bees quietly make it possible for flowers to become fruit. It gives them accurate, age-appropriate language for their own changing bodies and for how life continues in nature.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.domain: Description du fonctionnement et de la constitution d'objets techniques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to look at an everyday object, like a bike or a lamp, and figure out how it is built and why. They separate the need it answers from the job it does and the clever solution the designer chose, spot the materials and the groups of parts it is made of, and capture all this in simple sketches and diagrams. This trains them to see the design thinking hidden inside ordinary things.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to describe movement precisely: measuring how far something travels and how long it takes, and noticing whether it speeds up, slows down, or keeps a steady pace. A key idea is that motion depends on your point of view, so the same object can look like it is moving or standing still. They even stretch this to the sky, linking the length of a day to Earth spinning and the length of a year to Earth circling the Sun.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.domain: Démarche de conception et de réalisation d'un objet technique
clusters.summary: Your child is designing and building a real object to solve a problem, working through the full process from first idea to a finished mock-up they can test. Along the way they sketch solutions, choose materials for their properties, respect constraints like cost or safety, and weigh the environmental impact of their choices across the object's whole life. Because much of this happens in a team, they also practice planning, sharing tasks, and cooperating.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to think like a careful, questioning scientist: telling the difference between a belief and knowledge that has actually been established. They learn to ask which sources can be trusted, to check whether there is real evidence behind a claim, and to spot arguments that sound convincing but do not hold up. In a world full of information online, this is one of the most useful habits they can build.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.domain: La Terre, une planète singulière et active
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring what makes Earth able to support life, its place among the planets, and the forces that keep it active, from earthquakes and volcanoes to winds and ocean currents. They take real weather measurements and learn the crucial difference between day-to-day weather and long-term climate. Building on that, they use data to understand why the climate is warming today and what can be done to slow it or adapt to it.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.domain: Les objets techniques en réponse aux besoins des individus et de la société
clusters.summary: Your child is learning that the objects around us exist to meet human needs, and that those needs, and the tools that answer them, change over time and from place to place. They compare how a need like getting around is met differently in a city or the countryside, trace how objects evolve with history and technology, and even consider the ethical questions behind designing them. This connects science and technology to real society and everyday life.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring light, shadow, and the sky. They make and study shadows with opaque objects, sort materials by whether light passes through them clearly, dimly, or not at all, and follow the changing shapes of the Moon across its cycle. They also connect the changing seasons to how high the Sun sits and how long the days are, giving them a hands-on grasp of the light around them.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to measure and compare how heavy things are, sometimes with a simple balance they build themselves, and to weigh solids and liquids accurately by zeroing the scale. They practice converting between units of mass and volume, and begin to grasp density by noticing that two objects of the same size can have very different masses. These are the basic measuring skills that underpin all of physics and chemistry.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to use digital tools as part of doing science: writing and reading simple block-based programs, setting up a connected object, and using software to research, handle data, and share results. It introduces the logic of coding in a practical way and shows how computers help investigate and explain the world, not just for entertainment.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is putting numbers to movement: identifying whether motion is in a straight line or a curve, always stating the point of view they are using, and calculating speed from distance and time for steadily moving objects. They practice converting units of distance and time, including in the eye-opening context of planets orbiting the Sun. This turns an everyday idea like 'fast' into something they can measure and reason about.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is experimenting with mixtures: dissolving solids in water, recovering them by evaporation, discovering the point where no more will dissolve, and separating mixtures by sieving, settling, or filtering. They see that mass is conserved even when things are mixed, and that some mixtures react and truly change into something new. They also learn to read laboratory safety symbols and think about the environmental impact of the substances they handle.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is getting a big-picture view of life on Earth, past and present, and how astonishingly varied yet connected it all is. They classify organisms into nested groups by shared features, read simple family trees to see how species are related, use fossils to trace how life changed over deep time, and look at cells under a microscope to discover the tiny building block common to every living thing. It gives them a real sense of biodiversity and how scientists make sense of it.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.domain: Pratiquer des démarches scientifiques et technologiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to do science the way scientists actually do it: asking a clear question, forming a hypothesis that can be tested, designing and running an experiment, then interpreting the results and drawing supported conclusions. They also weigh how reliable their sources are, build models to explain how things work, and communicate their reasoning with evidence. These are the thinking and investigating skills at the heart of every science subject.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to program technical objects, such as making a robot follow a path or a streetlight switch on when someone passes. They trace how information flows from a sensor through a control box to an action like a motor or a light, write simple code to produce the behavior they want, and then test and improve it. It makes the automated devices around us feel understandable rather than magic.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring what materials are like and how they behave, watching matter change state as it heats and cools and measuring the temperatures at which that happens. They learn to tell materials found in nature from those made or transformed by humans, and research how long things like plastic, glass, or paper take to break down in the environment. This links a basic understanding of matter to real questions about waste and sustainability.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is building real electric circuits with a battery, switch, and bulb, adding sensors and motors, drawing proper circuit diagrams, and correcting the common idea that electricity gets 'used up' as it flows. They also learn how signals such as sound, light, and electricity carry information in everyday technology, and use models of the Sun, Earth, and Moon to explain shadows, eclipses, and the alternation of day and night.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering how living things depend on one another and on their surroundings within an ecosystem. They map who eats whom in food chains and webs, see why plants sit at the base as the producers, investigate what plants need to grow, and learn how dead matter is recycled by decomposers. They also examine how humans, the seasons, and natural disturbances change these environments, which grows into a real understanding of biodiversity and why resources must be used responsibly.

clusters.subject: Sciences et technologie
clusters.domain: États et constitution de la matière à l'échelle macroscopique
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to describe matter precisely, telling apart solids, liquids, and gases by their properties and measuring their mass and volume, including finding a solid's volume by how much liquid it pushes aside. They sort materials by traits like whether they conduct heat or electricity or respond to a magnet, watch substances change state at set temperatures, and separate liquids that do not mix. They also research how to handle household products safely and what impact those products have on the environment.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.domain: Comprendre, s'exprimer en utilisant la langue française à l'oral et à l'écrit
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to read and write French with real fluency, reaching for reading on their own and choosing precise words to say exactly what they mean. They practise the grammar and spelling rules, speak up clearly in front of others, and write organized texts they can go back and improve. This is the foundation for expressing themselves confidently in every other subject and in life.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.domain: Comprendre, s'exprimer en utilisant les langages mathématiques, scientifiques et informatiques
clusters.summary: Your child is learning the languages of maths and science: numbers, symbols, and the basics of coding that make computers work. They practise reading and building tables, graphs, and diagrams, and drawing the sketches and models scientists use to picture how things work. These skills help them describe the world precisely and make sense of data they meet everywhere.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.domain: Comprendre, s'exprimer en utilisant une langue étrangère et, le cas échéant, une langue régionale
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to understand and speak at least one foreign language (and often a second foreign or regional one), picking up not just words and pronunciation but the culture behind them. They practise joining conversations, listening to others, and adjusting how they speak to the situation. Opening up to other languages widens their horizons and builds real confidence in connecting with people.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to work as part of a team: sharing tasks, reaching agreement, helping classmates, and learning from them. They take on real projects, plan the steps, and check whether the goal was met, using digital tools to organize and share the work. These habits of cooperation prepare them for study and working life.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.domain: Domaine 5 : les représentations du monde et l'activité humaine
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how human societies work and how they came to be, from the great periods of history and founding events to the way we organize politics, economies, and shared spaces. They discover different cultures and art forms, the history of ideas and beliefs, and the scientific breakthroughs that changed how people live. This gives them the wide frame of reference needed to understand today's world and their place in it.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to think like a scientist: asking questions about what they observe, forming and testing ideas, and gathering evidence before drawing conclusions. They practise calculation and estimating whether an answer is sensible, solve problems involving quantities and proportions, and share their results clearly. This teaches a careful, curious way of reasoning that serves far beyond the science classroom.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.domain: Expression de la sensibilité et des opinions, respect des autres
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to name their feelings, believe in their own ability to succeed, and treat others with empathy and kindness. They practise settling disagreements through words rather than force, respecting opinions different from their own, and looking past stereotypes. This emotional and social groundwork helps them grow into a caring, self-assured person.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to imagine, design, and make things, whether an artwork, a hands-on production, or a sporting performance. They develop their taste and creativity, work within real constraints of materials and rules, and find their place in a group project alongside others. Creating and problem-solving this way builds initiative and the satisfaction of bringing an idea to life.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to find their way in space and time: reading and comparing maps, drawing their own, and understanding how looking at the past helps explain the present. They start to grasp the big questions of human development, such as inequalities and their causes. This gives them the bearings to make sense of where and when things happen in the world.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.summary: Your child is learning why rules and laws exist and how they protect everyone's freedom, from everyday classroom rules to the great declarations of human rights. They discover how citizens can shape and change the law, how the European Union works, and what laïcité means for living together peacefully. Understanding this helps them become a fair, engaged member of their community.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.domain: Médias, démarches de recherche et de traitement de l'information
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to find, sort, and judge information wisely, comparing sources and checking whether they can be trusted. They get to know the different media, how images and news are made, and how to use digital tools safely, legally, and respectfully. In a world flooded with information, these are essential skills for thinking clearly and not being misled.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.domain: Objectifs de connaissances et de compétences pour la maîtrise du socle commun
clusters.summary: Your child is building core knowledge of how the world works: numbers and shapes, energy and motion, the human body, the living world, and the structure of the universe from stars to the tiniest particles. They also learn how everyday technical objects work and how data and chance are handled. Together these give them a solid map of science and maths to build on for years to come.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how to learn: paying attention, memorizing, understanding what an instruction is really asking, and sticking with a task until it's done. They make their own study aids like notes and mind maps, plan their steps ahead of time, and treat mistakes as part of figuring things out. These study habits set them up to work independently and keep improving.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.summary: Your child is discovering great works of literature and art, from national and world heritage to today's creations, and learning to say and write what these works make them feel. They practise interpreting art and justifying their own creative choices, while also reading landscapes and linking places to how societies are organized. This nurtures both their sensitivity and their understanding of the human world.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.domain: Outils numériques pour échanger et communiquer
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to use digital tools to create, publish, and share work that combines text, images, and sound, and to collaborate online respectfully. They come to understand the difference between public and private, what a digital identity is, and the traces they leave behind, along with respecting copyright when reusing others' work. These are the everyday skills for being a thoughtful, safe digital citizen.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.domain: Responsabilité, sens de l'engagement et de l'initiative
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to take initiative and see projects through, weighing the consequences of their actions and keeping the commitments they make. They get involved in school life through collective projects and the simple tools of democracy, like agendas and votes. This grows their sense of responsibility and prepares them to act with purpose in the wider world.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to look after their own health and safety and to act responsibly toward others and the planet. They learn the basic safety rules of everyday life, how health can be affected by different risks, and why we need to protect natural resources and share them fairly. This awareness helps them make caring, well-informed choices for themselves and their community.

clusters.subject: Socle commun
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to think for themselves: telling fact from opinion, checking whether information holds up, and backing up their views with reasons. They practise debating moral and ethical questions, weighing others' arguments, and being willing to change their mind after a fair discussion. This builds the sound judgment and open-mindedness they'll rely on all their life.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.domain: Adopter un comportement éthique et responsable
clusters.summary: Your child is learning that the devices they use every day come with a hidden cost: the energy they consume, the environmental footprint of the materials inside them, and the impact of storing and moving data across networks. They are building habits for using connected devices responsibly, respecting other people's work and privacy along the way. At this age it helps them see technology as something with real consequences, not just something that magically works.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.domain: Analyser le fonctionnement et la structure d'un objet
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to take an everyday object apart in their mind, seeing it as a system with inputs, outputs and a job to do, and tracing the flows of material, energy and information that run through it. They discover the main families of materials, where energy comes from, and how a design is checked against what it was meant to do. This teaches them to look under the surface of the things around them and understand why they are built the way they are, all while working safely with real tools.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to invent, starting from a real need or problem, imagining possible solutions and building a working prototype to test whether an idea actually holds up. They work from a specification, match their ideas to the functions they need to fulfil, and even write and debug small programs, including for mobile apps. It is hands-on, creative problem-solving that turns a vague idea into something they have designed and made themselves.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.domain: Exprimer sa pensée à l'aide d'outils de description adaptés
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to get an idea out of their head and onto paper or a screen in a way others can understand, using freehand sketches, diagrams, mind maps and tree structures. They also meet the idea of an algorithm as a clear, ordered set of steps that solves a problem. Being able to describe and organise thinking clearly is a skill that pays off far beyond technology class.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.domain: Fonctions, solutions, constituants de la chaine d'énergie
clusters.summary: Your child is following the 'energy chain' inside a technical object, seeing how energy enters, gets transformed and flows through each part, from a battery to a switch to a motor. They learn the different forms energy takes and draw the whole thing out as a simple block diagram. This helps them understand what really makes a device move, light up or heat, rather than treating it as a black box.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.domain: Fonctions, solutions, constituants de la chaîne d'information
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how a smart object 'senses' the world, following the information chain from sensors that detect temperature, presence or distance through to the microcontroller that makes decisions. They discover how a real-world measurement gets turned into data a machine can actually use. This is the foundation for understanding the connected and automated objects that surround them.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.domain: Imaginer des solutions en réponse aux besoins, matérialiser une idée en intégrant une dimension design
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring how ideas become real projects with a design dimension, discovering connected objects, augmented reality, and what a consistent visual style (a 'charte graphique') brings to a project. They learn to work as a team with defined roles, plan ahead, and keep an eye on new technologies through the practice of 'veille'. It blends creativity, technology and teamwork in the way real design projects actually work.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to look at a technical object in context, describing how it interacts with its users and its surroundings and identifying the constraints and standards that shaped it. They pay attention to the user's experience, capturing how easy or pleasant an object is to use and moving from words and sketches toward more precise diagrams. This encourages them to think about people, not just parts, when judging a design.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is exploring how objects and technologies change over time, linking scientific discoveries to the inventions they made possible and spotting the breakthroughs that shift how we live. They meet the basics of artificial intelligence and its uses, learn to gather and analyse data, and think about how technical objects affect society. This gives them a sense of technology as an evolving story with real consequences, one they will help write.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.domain: La circulation de l'information dans un réseau informatique
clusters.summary: Your child is learning what actually happens when information travels across a network, from the devices involved (cards, switches, routers, servers) to the IP addresses that give each machine a unique identity. They see the difference between a home network and the wider Internet, meet the idea of protocols and routing, and even set up and troubleshoot a simple network in simulation. It demystifies the invisible plumbing behind every message and web page they use.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to run a technical project like a real designer: planning tasks over time, holding design reviews to check progress, and mapping out what depends on what with a planning diagram. Alongside this they think about a product's whole life cycle, its environmental impact, and the idea of eco-design and sustainable choices. It builds organisation and responsibility, showing that good technology is planned thoughtfully from start to finish.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to turn a design into a real part, using 3D modelling software (CAD) to shape it digitally and then choosing the right machine to make it, from a 3D printer to a laser cutter. They also learn to measure accurately and pick the right instrument to check their work. This is the modern version of the workshop, where a sketch becomes a physical object through digital tools.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to measure how well a technical object performs, defining what counts as performance (speed, energy autonomy and so on) and running protocols to measure it with the right instruments. They connect maths and physics to real devices, calculating with proper units and tracing where energy comes from and goes. It teaches them to judge objects objectively with evidence, and to weigh technology's benefits against its effect on the planet.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning the real building blocks of coding: variables, conditions, loops, lists, and how a program takes in data and produces results. They start with visual drag-and-drop blocks and move toward text-based programming, reading and modifying existing programs and translating between plain-language algorithms and working code. These are genuine, transferable programming skills that open the door to creating with technology rather than just using it.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.domain: Le choix d'un OST dans un contexte de développement durable
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to choose the right technical object thoughtfully, comparing options against criteria like performance, cost, repairability and carbon footprint rather than just picking what looks best. They learn to read the official French energy and repairability labels, understand a product's life cycle, and find their way around technical documentation. This turns them into a more informed, responsible consumer for life.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning why the material an object is made of matters, comparing properties like electrical and thermal conductivity to pick the one best suited to the job. They also weigh a material's carbon footprint, how available it is, and whether it can be recovered and recycled. It connects a practical engineering choice to a responsible environmental one.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to fix things rather than throw them away, diagnosing a faulty system and carrying out a repair, sometimes even making a custom replacement part. They get familiar with a fab-lab's tools and processes (3D printing, laser cutting, machining) and how materials behave under stress like bending or twisting, all while following workshop safety rules. This builds a repairer's mindset and real hands-on confidence.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to build prototypes that improve an object or add a new function, moving between sketches, schematics, models and digital representations to communicate a solution. Along the way they discover the mechanisms that transmit and transform motion, such as gears, belts and chains. It is where an idea becomes a tangible, testable object they can hold and refine.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.summary: Your child is learning how data is described, stored and processed, from choosing descriptors for an object to working with tables in a spreadsheet through calculations, sorting and filtering. They discover the bit as the tiny 0-or-1 unit of information and how text, numbers and images are encoded in binary. This gives them a grip on the raw material of the digital world they live in.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.domain: Usages et impacts sociétaux du numérique
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to navigate the digital world safely and responsibly, protecting personal data, organising files in folders, and understanding how information systems let people and machines share information. They confront serious issues too, such as cybersecurity, responsible online conduct and cyberviolence, and the environmental cost of our digital habits. These are the everyday digital-citizenship skills that matter enormously as they spend more time online.

clusters.subject: Technologie
clusters.domain: Écrire, mettre au point et exécuter un programme
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to program a real system, breaking a problem into smaller pieces and writing code that makes a physical object respond to events using sensors and actuators. They meet the idea of an embedded system tucked inside a controlled object, and learn to write, debug and check that their program does what they intended. It brings coding to life by connecting it to something that actually moves and reacts in the real world.

clusters.subject: Théâtre
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to watch theatre with a knowing eye, picking out how acting, staging, set, light, sound and costume come together to create a show. They start placing plays within the big periods of theatre history, discovering the many jobs behind a production, and reflecting on their own reactions as a spectator so they can talk about what they saw and why it moved them.

clusters.subject: Théâtre
clusters.summary: Your teenager is deepening their understanding of theatre as a live, one-of-a-kind event that only exists in the moment it is performed for an audience. They learn to analyse a play or a production with real precision, using the proper vocabulary, and to see how a show's artistic choices are tied to bigger questions about society, community and what it means to be human.

clusters.subject: Théâtre
clusters.summary: Your teenager is building the working habits of a young theatre student: researching in the school library or online to prepare their work, and writing or speaking clearly about the shows they have seen and the scenes they have rehearsed. They practise putting feelings into words and forming a reasoned personal opinion they can share and defend in group discussion.

clusters.subject: Théâtre
clusters.summary: Your teenager is sharpening how they study and discuss theatre, learning to read play texts and practitioners' writings, and to judge what a video recording of a performance can and cannot really capture. They present their own research to the class, argue a personal point of view in debate, and work on speaking in public with clarity and presence.

clusters.subject: Théâtre
clusters.summary: Your teenager is getting up on their feet and actually making theatre: improvising from a simple starting point, choosing a playing space that suits the scene, and offering different ways to bring a role to life. They learn to act with and truly listen to their partners, stay aware of the audience, and respond quickly when a director gives new instructions during rehearsal.

clusters.subject: Théâtre
clusters.summary: Your teenager is growing into a more independent performer, throwing themselves into unfamiliar acting challenges and making thoughtful, deliberate choices on stage. They commit fully to collective work, contribute to a full production (acting, and possibly scenography, sound, lighting or video too), and learn to analyse their own performance and their partners' in light of everything they know about theatre.

clusters.subject: Théâtre
clusters.summary: Your teenager is travelling through the whole history of Western theatre, from ancient Greek tragedy and medieval town festivals to the great classical and 19th-century forms, and on into modern and contemporary stage work. Along the way they see how the written text and everything beyond it (set, light, sound, video) together shape a production, giving them a rich frame for the plays they study and perform.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to work like a real musician across the whole creative arc: exploring sounds and planning a project, performing and inventing music alone and with others, and then listening closely to compare and comment on what they hear. Just as important, they learn to share their own work and defend their opinions in respectful, reasoned discussion. These are the core habits that turn a music class into genuine artistic practice.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to sing in tune, from memory, and with real feeling, building a repertoire of songs and famous works over the year. They begin holding their own part in a two-voice song, use their voice and body to bring out a mood, and pick up the vocabulary to talk about how music is expressed. This is where singing shifts from simply repeating a tune to genuinely interpreting it.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.domain: Développer une écoute comparée, analytique et critique
clusters.summary: Your teenager is learning to listen like a critic: describing music with precise vocabulary, comparing works to spot what makes each one distinctive, and placing unfamiliar pieces within the broad currents of Western music history. Starting from a personal impression, they build it into a reasoned, well-argued opinion. This trains the kind of careful listening and clear thinking that carries far beyond the music room.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your teenager is discovering the pleasure of making music together, from performing to inventing, while building an organized sense of how music has developed over time. They also learn to connect music to other art forms and other subjects they study. The goal is both enjoyment and a real, structured musical culture they can draw on.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your teenager is deepening their musical culture by listening to and comparing works from many eras and places, while developing their own expression across different styles. Along the way they build self-control, attention to detail, and the confidence to take creative risks and make their own artistic choices. It is as much about growing as a person as it is about music.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your child is becoming a sound explorer and a young composer, experimenting with voices, everyday objects, and nature, then sorting, choosing, and arranging sounds into their own short pieces. They even invent picture-based scores to write down and remember what they create. Working in a group teaches them to concentrate, listen to others, and respect everyone's ideas.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your child is creating and reworking music with more independence, arranging existing pieces, composing in the style of a known work, and building purposeful playlists, often with the help of digital tools. They learn to plan a project, find the resources it needs, and improve it step by step while honestly assessing their own progress. Creating themselves also makes them sharper, more generous listeners to what others make.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.domain: La diversité des esthétiques, des langages et des techniques de la création musicale dans le temps et dans l'espace
clusters.summary: Your teenager is exploring the big questions of what music actually is by examining how it connects to images, text, movement, and even noise and language. They compare art music with popular music, old works with contemporary ones, and music passed down in writing with music passed down by ear. These contrasts open up how varied and rich musical creation has been across time and cultures.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.domain: La place de la musique et de ses pratiques dans la société contemporaine
clusters.summary: Your teenager is thinking about the place of music in today's world: how we listen to it, where it is played, and how digital technology, globalization, and commerce reshape it. They weigh music as personal taste against music as shared heritage, and consider the musician's role and the many purposes music serves. It is a chance to look critically at something they already live with every day.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.domain: La place de la musique et de ses pratiques dans la société contemporaine
clusters.summary: Your teenager is examining how music fits into modern life, comparing the many ways we now listen to it, from earbuds on the move to a live concert, and reflecting on how digital tools and algorithms change how music is made and shared. They also consider music as both a personal taste and a collective heritage. The aim is a thoughtful, informed view of the musical world around them.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.domain: Le son, la musique, l'espace et le temps
clusters.summary: Your teenager is exploring how music works and where it lives, from the science and acoustics behind sound to the spaces and buildings that shape how we hear it. They study how music carries a story, and how musical language is organized into form and time. This ties musical listening to physics, architecture, and storytelling all at once.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your young child is discovering visual art by drawing, painting, and building in three dimensions, exploring colors, tools, and materials like clay, paper, and cardboard. They build up a store of images and patterns to reuse and reinvent, and begin telling apart a real thing from a picture of it. All this hands-on making also trains careful looking and the fine hand control that later helps with writing.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.domain: Objectifs visés et éléments de progressivité
clusters.summary: Your young child is developing a genuine taste for the arts, discovering around ten artworks from different times and kinds, and learning to put words to their feelings and choices. They start to compare what they made with what they meant to make. Talking about art, before, during, and after, becomes a natural part of every activity.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your teenager is connecting their musical practice to the wider world through documented projects that pair a performance with real research, and by learning to share music with an audience. They also discover the many jobs in the music economy, from live shows to distribution, and how music works socially and financially. This helps them see where their interests could lead after school.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.domain: Réaliser des projets musicaux d'interprétation ou de création
clusters.summary: Your child is carrying out real musical projects, whether performing existing pieces or creating their own, and holding their own part when singing together in harmony. They work with a wide repertoire, use digital tools to record and shape sound, and even explore how words fit a melody. They also learn to present their work to others and explain the artistic choices behind it.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your young child is discovering the world of sound by singing nursery rhymes and songs, playing simple instruments, and experimenting with all the sounds their voice and body can make. They listen ever more closely, remember and reproduce simple rhythms, and learn to sing together as a group. Sorting instruments by how they are played gives them their first way of organizing what they hear.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to say what they think about music and back it up with reasons, rather than just reacting with "I like it" or "I don't." They pick up the words needed to express their taste and develop a critical ear, while listening to and respecting classmates whose tastes differ. This is early practice in forming and defending an opinion with kindness.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to debate music thoughtfully, contributing to group decisions and separating what they personally like from what a piece objectively does. They take a constructive, critical look at their own and others' work, frame their listening around a guiding question, and even respect sources and copyright when using sounds. Conclusions from these debates then feed directly into their next musical project.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.summary: Your child is learning to listen actively, describing and comparing music from many cultures and eras and spotting simple patterns like a repeated melody or theme. They begin placing a piece in its time and place, recognizing vocal and instrumental forms, and getting to know the symphony orchestra and its instrument families. A growing vocabulary lets them talk precisely about pitch, tone, and rhythm, and even link music to history, geography, and science.

clusters.subject: Éducation musicale / Musique
clusters.domain: Écouter, comparer, construire une culture musicale et artistique
clusters.summary: Your teenager is building a real musical culture: analyzing works with precise vocabulary, comparing different interpretations of the same piece, and setting up landmarks across the history of music and the arts. They explore the science of sound and hearing (including how to protect it), the main styles and categories of music, and what digital tools bring to creating and sharing it. They also glimpse the professional world of music and how it connects to other arts and society.
